name: HMS Unison (P 43)
events: The history of HMS P 43 / HMS Unison as compiled on this page is extracted from the patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine and was created by us in October 2014. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in June 2026.

5 Feb 1942 P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) was commissioned for trials at Barrow. She was not yet taken over from the shipyard, this would only be done on completion of the acceptance trials which would. This would usually take place on the day of her arrival at Holy Loch. (1)

18 Feb 1942 Around 1345 A/18, P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Barrow for Holy Loch. She made the passage together with HMS Graph (Lt.Cdr. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN). They were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN).
Earlier that day, P 43 had conducted compass adjustment trials at Barrow. (1)

19 Feb 1942 Around 1545 A/19, P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived at Holy Loch to begin a period of trials and training. On arrival at Holy Loch she was taken over from the shipyard and placed in full commission. (1)
21 Feb 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted independent exercises in Loch Long. Also fuel consumption trials were carried out on the measured half mile. (1)
22 Feb 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted independent exercises in Loch Long. (1)
23 Feb 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted exercises and a deep dive trial in the Clyde area. (1)
24 Feb 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted independent exercises in the Clyde area. (1)
27 Feb 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted gunnery exercises and D/F calibration trials in the Clyde area. (1)
28 Feb 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (1)
1 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Arrochar. (2)
2 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (2)
3 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (2)
4 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) proceeded from Arrochar to Holy Loch. (2)
8 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Ardrossan where she was immediately docked. (2)
11 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) was undocked at Ardrossan. She returned to Holy Loch later the same day. (2)
12 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted exercises in Loch Long. These were followed by D/G trials off Helensburgh. (2)
13 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Arrochar. (2)
14 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. Upon completion of these trials HMS P 43 returned to Holy Loch. (2)
16 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS Z 5 (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR) acted as target. (2)
17 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS Albrighton (Lt. R.J. Hanson, RN) acted as target. (2)
19 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS Z 5 (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR) acted as target. (2)
21 Mar 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted night attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HNoMS Draug acted as target. (2)

22 Mar 1942 Around 0715 A/22, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Holy Loch for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol to the north-east of the Shetland Islands for an anti U-Boat patrol.
On departed from Holy Loch attack exercises were carried out during which HNoMS Draug acted as target.
At 1215 A/22, rendezvous was made with HMS P 511 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) and their escort, HMS Convolvulus (T/Lt. R.C. Connell, RNR).
Course was then set towards the Minches.
Around 0840 A/24, off Scapa Flow, HMS P 511 parted company. (3)
22 Mar 1942 For the daily positions of HMS P 43 during her 1st war patrol see the map below.
24 Mar 1942 At 2025 A/24, in position 090° - Bard Head - 5 miles, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) parted company with her escort, HMS Convolvulus (T/Lt. R.C. Connell, RNR).
P 43 was then to proceed through position 61°28'N, 00°32'W and then to proceed (diving by day) to patrol within 30 miles of position 62°20'N, 01°00'E. (4)

27 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1527 A/27 the Capt. S.3 sent the following orders to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); Leave patrol after dark/30 so as to pass through position 61°59'N, 00°22'E at 2200 A/30, thence on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion through positions 61°29'N, 00°31'W and 090° - Outskerries - 5 miles postition 090° - Bardhead - 5 miles. E.T.A. in this last position at 1400 A/31 to await onward escort into Lerwick. H.Q.C.C. [Headquarters Coastal Command] is requested to arrange total bombing restrictions 30 miles either side of lines joining position the positions above from 2100 A/30 to 1800 A/31. (5)

30 Mar 1942 At 1450 A/30, in position 61°57'N, 01°04'E, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) sighted a vessel bearing 070° at a range of 2.5 miles in moderate visibility. An attack was started and all tubes were brought to the ready.
At 1459 A/30, when the range had come down to 1100 yards the vessel was identified as the Swedish Glimmaren (3173 GRT, built 1939) proceeding at a speed of 14 knots. The attack was broken off.
At 2125 A/30, HMS P 43 surfaced after a day of submerged patrol. She then left patrol and course was set to return to Lerwick in accordance with the Capt. S.3's signal timed 1527 A/27. (3)
31 Mar 1942 Around 2030 A/31, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Lerwick.
She was escorted in by an M/S drifter [identity not known] which had joined around 1950 A/31 in position 250° - Bard Head - 3.5 miles. (3)

3 Apr 1942 Around 1500 A/3, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Lerwick for Holy Loch. She made the passage together with HMS Tuna (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN). They were escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN). (6)
5 Apr 1942 Around 1900 A/5, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Lerwick.
6 Apr 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (6)
14 Apr 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (6)
16 Apr 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Ardrossan where she was immediately docked. She was undocked later the same day. (6)
17 Apr 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) proceeded from Ardrossan to Holy Loch. (6)
18 Apr 1942 Around 1230 A/18, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Holy Loch for Lerwick but she first carried out some exercises.

Around 1900 A/18, she made rendezvous with HMS Sturgeon (Lt.Cdr. M.R.G. Wingfield, RN) which was also to proceed to Lerwick. The submariens were escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN). (6)
20 Apr 1942 Around 2100 A/20, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived at Lerwick from Holy Loch. (6)
22 Apr 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted special trials off Lerwick with aircraft. (6)

24 Apr 1942 At 2200 A/24, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Lerwick for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to provide cover during convoy operations (PQ 15 and QP 11) to and from Northern Russia.
HMS P 43 was to pass through position 090° - Out Skerries - 5 miles and then direct to her patrol area ('K 26').
For the daily positions of HMS P 43 during this patrol see the map below.

27 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 0113 B/27, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) informed HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HNoMS Uredd (Lt. Røren), ORP Jastrzab (Lt.Cdr. B. Romanowski) and FFS Minerve (Lt. P.M. Sonneville) of the following; A submarine was sighted by an aircraft at 1444 B/26 on a northerly course leaving a zigzag oil track about 100 feet wide and 3 miles long in approximate position 62°49'N, 00°39'E.
This however, had not been a German submarine as the submarine in question was Minerve which reported diving for an unidentified twin-enginged aircraft in position 62°24'N, 00°06'E. (7)

29 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1641 B/29, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HNoMS Uredd (Lt. Røren), ORP Jastrzab (Lt.Cdr. B. Romanowski) and FFS Minerve (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); At 0800 B/30, P 43, P 551 (Jastrzab), Urred and Minerve move so as to be in zones 'K 30', 'K 28', 'K 26', 'K 24' respectively at 0800 B1. (8)

30 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1658 B/30, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Sturgeon (Lt. M.R.G. Wingfield, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HNoMS Uredd (Lt. Røren), ORP Jastrzab (Lt.Cdr. B. Romanowski) and FFS Minerve (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); 1) On reaching 05°00'E estimated to be about 1200 A/1 Sturgeon is to leave convoy PQ 15 and proceed with despatch to patrol in zone 'K 26'. Total bombing restrictions are to be in force 30 miles either side of a line joining positions 71°58'N, 05°02'E and 70°20'N, 07°30'E from 0800 A/1 to 1800 A/2. 2) At 0800 A/1, P 43, P 551, Uredd and Minerve move so as to be in zones 'K 34', 'K 32', 'K 30' and 'K 28' respectively, at 0800 A/2. (8)

2 May 1942 In a signal timed 2009 A/2, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Sturgeon (Lt. M.R.G. Wingfield, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), ORP Jastrzab (Lt.Cdr. B. Romanowski), HNoMS Uredd (Lt. Røren) and FFS Minerve (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); At 1200 A/3, P 43, P 551, Uredd, Minerve and Sturgeon are to move so as to be in zones 'K 28', 'K 26', 'K 24', 'K 22' and 'K 20' respectively by 1200 A/4. Submarines are subsequently to leave patrol at 1200 A/4 for Lerwick. Estimated positions of the convoy at 0800 A/3 are as follows; PQ 15 in 73°50'N, 24°00'E and QP 11 in 71°30'N, 06°00'E. (9)

3 May 1942 In a signal timed 1115 B/3, the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to HMS Sturgeon (Lt. M.R.G. Wingfield, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HNoMS Uredd (Lt. Røren), ORP Jastrzab (Lt.Cdr. B. Romanowski) and FFS Minerve (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); 1) Leave patrol at 1200 B/4 for Lerwick routed as follows; P 43 through positions 'A' (70°10'N, 08°20'E) and 'B' (090° - Out Skerries - 5 miles), thence to Lerwick. The remaining submarines are to proceed direct to position 'B' thence to Lerwick. 2) The submarines are to proceed on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion and are to adjust their speeds so as to arrive at Lerwick as follows; Sturgeon 2000 B/6, Minerve 0600 B/7, Uredd 1100 B/7, Jastrzab 1600 B/7 and P 43 2200 B/7. (10)

5 May 1942 In a signal the Senior Officer Lerwick sent the following orders to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HNoMS Uredd (Lt. Røren), ORP Jastrzab (Lt.Cdr. B. Romanowski) and FFS Minerve (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); Make rendezvous with the local escort, drifter HMS Harmattan, in position 090° - Bard Head - 5 miles. Escort will be in position for Minerve at 0500 B/7 until met, for Uredd at 0900 B/7 until met, for Jastrzab at 1400 B/7 until met and for P 43 at 1900 B/7 until dark. (10)
7 May 1942 At 1900 B/7, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Lerwick.
She was escorted in by the M/S drifter HMS Harmattan (T/Lt. J.E. Polmont, RNVR) which had joined around 1800 B/7. (3)

8 May 1942 Around 0700 B/8, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Lerwick for Holy Loch. She made the passage together with HMS Sturgeon (Lt.Cdr. M.R.G. Wingfield, RN). They were escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN). (11)
10 May 1942 Around 1000 B/10, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Lerwick. (11)
14 May 1942 Around 2135 B/14, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Holy Loch for Gibraltar.

Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN) and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN). They were escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR).
The escort parted company with the submarines near Bishop Rock around 2115 B/16.
P 43 was routed as follows; Diving by day through position 'E' (49°20'N, 13°02'E) to position 'F' (42°59'N, 13°02'E) where she was to arrive at 0530 B/23.
For the daily positions of HMS P 43 during this passage see the map below.

16 May 1942 In a signal timed 1310 B/16, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); You are to proceed on the surface at discretion of the Commanding Officer from 43°00'N, through positions 'A' (37°30'N, 10°00'E) and 'B' (225° - Cape St. Vincent - 15 miles) to make rendezvous with the escort in position 'C' (350° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles. Traveller should pass position 'A' at 1100 B/23 and position 'B' at 1630 B/23 and arrive in position 'C' at 0730 B/24. P 43 should pass position 'A' at 0300 B/25, position 'B' at 1030 B/25 and arrive in position 'C' at 0730 B/26. If unable to comply within two hours you should report. Bombing restrictions have been arranged. (13)

20 May 1942 In a signal timed 1712 B/20, D.D.I.C. [Deputy Director Intelligence Centre] sent the following information to HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN), HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); By D/F at 1409 Z/20 a U-boat within 40 miles of position 47°30'N, 14°30'W, probably east bound.

This signal referred to the German submarine U-582 which had send a signal at 1607 B/20 from naval grid BE 6525 (centred in position 47°15'N, 13°45'W) reporting having sighted a submarine that morning. The submarine she had sighted was the Italian Reginaldo Giuliani which had also sighted the German submarine. (14)
24 May 1942 At 0812 B/24, in position 39°37'N, 11°00'W, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) dived for a ship that was sighted on bearing 195°. An attack was started.
At 0820 B/24, the attack was broken off when the ship was bearing 280° at 2000 yards. It was identified as a Portugese coaster of about 700 tons.
At 2249 B/24, in approximate position 38°00'N, 10°20'W, HMS P 43 passed a tanker burning navigation lights at a range of 7 miles. It is thought this may have been a Vichy-French tanker. (15)
26 May 1942 At 1015 B/26, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Holy Loch.
She was escorted in by HMS Lady Hogarth (T/Lt. S.G. Barnes, RNR) which had joined around 0715 B/26 in position 250° - Cape Spartel - 6 miles. (12)
2 Jun 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar together with HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN) and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN). (16)

4 Jun 1942 Around 1700 B/4, P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to form a patrol line between Sardinia and Sicily with HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) to cover operation Harpoon.
Patrol positions were as follows for these four submarines; P 42 38°10'N, 11°50'E. P 43 38°55'N, 09°40'E. P 44 38°30'N, 11°10'E. P 211 38°45'N, 10°25'E.

P 42 and P 43 were the two submarines that were to patrol the nearest to the coasts of Sicily and Sardinia respectively and were ordered to attack only enemy cruisers and above and enemy submarines until dawn 17th when it was estimated that the convoys would have arrived and that the covering forces were retiring.
P 44 and P 211 were allowed to attack southbound merchant ships over 2000 tons but were to retain a full salvo for enemy cruisers and above and enemy submarines.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 43 during this patrol see the map below.
Timespan: 12 to 18 June 1942.

During March and April 1942 Malta had been attacked very heavily by the German and Italian air forces and was in much need of supplies. It was therefore decided that two convoy’s were to be sent, one from the west (Harpoon) and one from the east (Vigorous). This was to increase the chance of success as the enemy would have to split force if they want to attack both convoys. Also a group of minesweepers were to be sent to Malta.
Below we will give the events regarding the Harpoon convoy in chronological order.
Western Mediterranean (Harpoon convoy)

During the night convoy WS 19 Z passed the Straits of Gibraltar. This convoy had departed the Clyde on June 6th. It was made up of five merchant vessels; Burwan (British , 6069 GRT, built 1928), Chant (American, 5601 GRT, built 1938), Orari (British, 10350 GRT, built 1931), Tanimbar (Dutch, 8169 GRT, built 1930) and Troilus (British, 7422 GRT, built 1921).
Off Gibraltar the tanker Kentucky (American , 9308 GRT, built 1942) joined the convoy.

Close escort was provided by ‘Force X’ which was made up of the AA-cruiser HMS Cairo (A/Capt. C.C. Hardy, DSO, RN), destroyers HMS Bedouin (Cdr. B.G. Scurfield, OBE, RN), HMS Marne (Lt.Cdr. H.N.A. Richardson, DSC, RN), HMS Matchless (Lt.Cdr. J. Mowlam, RN), HMS Partridge (Lt.Cdr. W.A.F. Hawkins, DSC, OBE, RN), HMS Ithuriel (Lt.Cdr. D.H. Maitland-Makgill-Crichton, DSC, RN), escort destroyers HMS Badsworth (Lt. G.T.S. Gray, DSC, RN), HMS Blankney (Lt.Cdr. P.F. Powlett, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Middleton (Lt.Cdr. D.C. Kinloch, RN), ORP Kujawiak (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) L. Lichodziejewski), minesweepers HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RN), HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN), HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RN), HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) and the motor launches (ML’s) ML 121 (group commander Lt.Cdr. E.J. Strowlger, RNVR), ML 134, ML 135, ML 168, ML 459 and ML 462.
Also operating with ‘Force X’ was the fast minelayer HMS Welshman (Capt. W.H.D. Friedberger, RN) which was to carry stores and personnel to Malta.

Distant cover was provided by ‘Force W’ which was made up of the battleship HMS Malaya (Capt. J.W.A. Waller, RN), aircraft carriers HMS Eagle (Capt. E.G.N. Rushbrooke, DSC, RN), HMS Argus (Capt. G.T. Philip, DSC, RN), light cruisers HMS Kenya (Capt. A.S. Russell, RN, flying the flag of Vice-Admiral A.T.B. Curteis, CB, RN), HMS Liverpool (Capt. W.R. Slayter, DSC, RN), AA-cruiser HMS Charybdis (Capt. L.D. Mackintosh, DSC, RN), destroyers HMS Onslow (Capt. H.T. Armstrong, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Icarus (Lt.Cdr. C.D. Maud, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Escapade (Lt.Cdr. E.N.V. Currey, DSC, RN), HMS Antelope (Lt.Cdr. E.N. Sinclair, RN), HMS Wishart (Cdr. H.G. Scott, RN), HMS Westcott (Cdr. I.H. Bockett-Pugh, DSO, RN), HMS Wrestler (Lt. R.W.B. Lacon, DSC, RN) and HMS Vidette (Lt.Cdr. E.N. Walmsley, DSC, RN). This force was to cover the convoy until off the Skerki Channel, the entrance to the Sicily-Tunis Narrows. The cover forces for this convoy were however rather weak. For instance the aircraft carriers were rather old and had hardly enough fighters available to provide a decent air patrol.

Then there was also a tanker force to fuel the escorts ‘Force Y’. It was made up of the RFA oiler Brown Ranger (3417 GRT, built 1941), escorted by two corvettes; HMS Geranium (T/Lt. A. Foxall, RNR) and HMS Coltsfoot (T/Lt. the Hon. W.K. Rous, RNVR).

Besides these forces four submarines were on patrol in the western Mediterranean. They were stationed between Sardinia and Sicily. These were HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN).
By 0800 B/12 the force was in full strength and proceeded eastwards at 12 to 13 knots.
The remainder of the day was uneventful except for the sighting of a Spanish merchant vessel in the evening.
On this day the convoy was shadowed continuously by German and Italian aircraft. Also it was thought an Italian submarine might have spotted the convoy but was not the case as of yet.
HMS Cairo and almost all the destroyers and escort destroyers oiled from Brown Ranger and HMS Liverpool. This was completed late in the evening.
Italian warships reported to be at sea.

Two Italian cruisers and five destroyers had been reported at daybreak (actually six detroyers were present). These were the light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia, Raimondo Montecuccoli and the destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Vincenzo Gioberti, Ascari, Ugolino Vivaldi, Nicolò Zeno and Premuda. They had sailed on the 13th from Cagliari, Sardinia. The most western British submarine on patrol HMS P 43 had attacked them at 1931 hours on the 13th. She claimed to have hit a cruiser but this was obviously not the case. Two hours later the next submarine on the patrol line HMS P 211 also sighted this Italian force but was too far off to attack.

During the night the force was spotted and reported by an Italian submarine. In fact two Italian submarines made attacks on the convoy during the night. These were the Uarsciek at 0152 B/14 which fired two torpedoes at a destroyer in position 38°02'N, 05°06'E. Both torpedoes missed. Then at 0505 B/14, the Giada fired four torpedoes at an aircraft carrier (probably HMS Eagle although this carrier did not report hearing torpedo explosions and HMS Argus did) and a cruiser or battleship in position 37°55'N, 06°12'E. She claimed two hits but in fact all torpedoes missed.

At dawn enemy shadowing aircraft appeared once more. The convoy was approaching the danger area for air attacks coming from Sardinia. At 1000 B/12 the first radar warning came and at about the same time fighters from Eagle shot down an Italian torpedo aircraft. More of these aircraft were seen gathering about 20 miles from the convoy and form up for attack.

It was a bright and clear morning with hardly a cloud in the sky. There was little wind but such as there was came from the west and this made it difficult for the British fighter crews, especially for those from the 25-year old Argus with her small margin of speed, unless she would turn into the wind and leave the destroyer screen.

The convoy was steering east in two columns in line ahead. HMS Kenya was leading the port column while HMS Liverpool was leading the starboard one. Astern of the convoy was HMS Malaya with HMS Welshman astern of her. The aircraft carriers were operating independently to port of the convoy. Each carrier had an AA cruiser and a destroyer as escort. HMS Eagle was with HMS Cairo and HMS Wishart while HMS Argus was with HMS Charybdis and HMS Vidette.

The remaining fifteen destroyers and four minesweepers formed an all-round screen spread from three to three and a half miles from the convoy. This was done on purpose so that all ships could fire outward but also inward with a freedom that would have been impossible with a closer screen.

The air attacks began at 1030 B/14. The first was a shallow dive-bombing attack by two groups, each of four or five Italian fighter-bombers (CR. 42). One group approached from astern at 12000 feet and diving to 6000 feet. The other group came from ahead at 6000 feet and dropped their bombs from 3000 to 4000 feet. Their target was HMS Argus and her consorts on the port beam of HMS Malaya. No damage was done, only one bomb fell close to HMS Charybdis. Two of the enemy planes were shot down after their attack by Fulmar’s from Eagle which were controlled by the Argus and afterwards landed aboard her. It was the policy to employ the Hurricanes from Eagle as high fighter force and the Fulmar’s from Argus as low fighter force.

A much more serious attack followed half an hour later when 28 Savoia torpedo aircraft escorted by 20 Macchi fighters conducted a combined attack with 10 Cant. high level bombers. The Savoia approached from the northward in two waves of equal strength. The first wave came in at 1110 B/14 and the second soon afterwards. The first wave passed through the destroyer screen at 500 feet above the water, rounded the rear of the convoy, and attacked from the starboard side, splitting into groups before firing. They dropped their torpedoes from a height of 100 feet at a range of 2000 yards. They hit HMS Liverpool, which was leading the starboard column, when she was turning to meet the attack. Also the Dutch merchant Tanimbar was hit in the rear and she sank within a few minutes in position 36°58’N, 07°30’E.

The second wave attacked the port column dropped their torpedoes at longer range. All torpedoes missed. The Cant. bombers also came in two formations, coming from ahead out of the sun at a height of about 10000 feet. Their targets seemed to be Eagle and Argus but none of their bombs hit.

A little before 1200 B/14 several torpedo planes made harmless attacks from long range. They were probably stragglers turned back by gunfire during the earlier attacks and anxious to get rid of their torpedoes before turning back to base.

Upon the whole the Italians seem to have attacked gallantly. The British fighters claimed to have shot down three enemy fighters and three torpedo aircraft. Three British fighters were lost ofwhich one was shot down in error by a ship in the screen. The convoy and escort claim to have shot down seven enemy aircraft, all Savoia SM 79’s.

HMS Liverpool was hit in the engine room and badly damaged. She could only make 3 to 4 knots on one shaft. She was ordered to return to Gibraltar being towed by HMS Antelope and screened by HMS Westcott. A long voyage during which the first 24 hours she was attacked from the air. At 1640 B/14, five CR. 42 fighter-bombers attacked from astern out of the sun, luckily without hitting, though one or two bombs fell close enough to increase the ships list. At 1800 B/14, the tow having parted, there was a harmless attempt by eleven high-level bombers followed by an equally harmless attempt by seven torpedo aircraft which were heavily escorted by fighters. The Liverpool and Westcott each claimed to have destroyed a torpedo plane.
At 2015 B/14, now once more in tow, fife high-level bombers attacked but their bombs fell wide.
At 2230 B/14, six torpedo bombers made a twilight attack from very long range only to loose one of their number to the barrage HMS Liverpool put up.

The fruitless attacks on the damaged Liverpool in the afternoon and evening of the 14th evidently occupied the remaining aircraft available to the enemy in Sardinia for as the convoy was able to continue without being attacked. It was however still being shadowed and came within range of the Sicilian air bases in the evening.

HMS Welshman had replaced HMS Liverpool at the head of the starboard column of the convoy. She however parted company with the convoy around 2000 B/14 to continue the passage to Malta on her own at high speed.

At 1820 B/14 German bombers appeared, about ten Ju. 88’s approached the convoy from astern at 10000 feet and then dived to 6000 feet to make the attack. Both carriers had narrow escapes, Argus in particular. A bomb pitched fine on her port bow, dived under the ship and exploded on the starboard bow. No ship was damaged however. No enemy aircraft were shot down. Six British fighters however harassed the enemy and forced several of them to release their bombs prematurely. One Fulmar was lost.

As in the morning the shallow dive-bombing attack preluded a heavy combined torpedo and bombing attack but in the evening the lapse of time was greater and dive-bombers as well as high level-bombers took part in the massed attack. It was a combination of Italians and Germans. 16 Savoia 79 bombers heavily escorted by Macchi fighters with 10 Ju 88’s and 15 Ju 87’s. The first to appear were the Savoia’s which approached from the north-east to port at about 2000 B/14. They were flying well above the water. Worked their way around the stern of the convoy outside gun range to glide down and attack on the starboard side. In the meantime, a few minutes after the Savoia’s had been sighted, two groups of Ju 88’s came in from ahead at 12000 feet and dropped their bombs without effect as they flew across the screen and along the columns of the convoy. Next the Ju 87’s arrived on the port bow and attacked the port wing of the screen, diving from 7000 to 1000 feet. They narrowly missed HMS Icarus and HMS Wrestler, though they had probably hoped to reach HMS Eagle. These dive bombers took most of the attention of the screen but then at 2020 B/14 the Italian torpedo-bombers came in. Most of them concentrated onHMS Malaya, HMS Argus, HMS Charybdis and HMS Vidette. They managed to drop three torpedoes within 300 yards from the carrier but she still managed to avoid them.

Around the time of these attacks HMS Middleton sighted a periscope and dropped a depth charge. Two other destroyers then hauled out of the screen and dropped depth charges. The periscope was next sighted by HMS Malaya after which HMS Speedy obtained an Asdic contact and attacked with depth charges in position 37°39’N, 09°35’E, claiming to have destroyed the enemy submarine.
This was the last encounter with the enemy before ‘Force W’ would separate from the convoy which was then to continue on to Malta only escorted by ‘Force X’.

As the convoy reached the entrance of the Narrows at 2100 B/14, four Beaufighters arrived from Malta to relieve the hard worked naval aviators of the carriers. Around this time the Italian submarine Alagi attacked an aircraft carrier with two stern torpedoes in position 37°36'N, 09°53'E which both missed. The attack was not reported by either of the carriers and was probably not observed. Half an hour later ‘Force W’ turned westwards. The convoy continued eastwards with A/Capt. Hardy of HMS Cairo in command. For the passage of the Tunisian coast the five remaining merchant vessels formed a single line ahead with ‘Force X’ screening them.

At 2205 B/14, as it was getting dark, eight Ju 88’s made a shallow dive-bombing attack dropping down from 6000 to 3000 feet to release their bombs. No hits were obtained. They lost two aircraft, one was shot down by a Beaufighter and the ther by gunfire from the ships. This was the end of this day’s fighting.
The Italian ships that had been reported to be at sea the previous day.

On receiving the submarines reports Vice-Admiral Leatham at Malta arranged for a striking force of Wellington aircraft to attack the enemy. Aircraft again sighted the enemy north-west of Cape San Vito, Sicily at 0255/14. At 0525/14 the enemy was sighted off Palermo. At 1800/14 two cruisers were reported to be in the harbour there. At dusk, at 2125 B/14, two cruisers and four destroyers were reported to be leaving Palermo harbour but their course was not reported. Vice-Admiral Leatham judged that they were proceeding to the east to join the main Italian battlefleet that had left Taranto that same evening to operate against the ‘Vigorous-convoy’ in the eastern Mediterranean. Accordingly he stationed a naval air patrol over the Strait of Messina, with a naval air striking force at Malta standing by to attack.
‘Force W’

Vice-Admiral Curteis, who was taking ‘Force W’ westwards, also received the report of the enemy leaving Palermo and had to decide whether to strengthen ‘Force X’ with either one or both his cruisers, HMS Kenya and HMS Charybdis. He was then, at 2315/24, in position 37°30’N, 09°30’E, over 50 nautical miles from the convoy, which would be a further 100 nautical miles further on to the east by dawn on the 15th. He also judged that the Italian ships would be unlikely to be danger to the convoy and that the escort would be strong enough ‘to deter them from doing any harm’ escpecially as it would be expected that the Italians would be attacked from the air by aircraft from Malta. Apart from this he was anxious for the safety of his aircraft carriers, which would need the cruisers support while within striking distance from the enemy air bases in Sardinia. Furthermore there was barely time to overtake the convoy before by the morning. With the force available a decision either way was a gamble this might have been different had Liverpool not been torpedoed. He therefore decided against sending any reinforcement to the convoy.
Action south of Pantellaria

A/Capt. Hardy, the convoy escort commander in HMS Cairo first knew of the presence of the enemy through the report of a Beaufighter which was on it’s way to patrol above the convoy and which at 0620 B/15 reported two cruisers and four destroyers to be 15 nautical miles on the port beam of the convoy. The convoy at that time was stearing at 12 knots to the south-east. The merchantmen were formed in two columns again, with HMS Cairo ahead, the five ‘Fleet’ destroyers in the screen to starboard and the four ‘Hunt’s’ to port. The minesweepers and the ML’s were astern of the convoy. A few minutes later the Italian ships were sighted hull down against the brightening sky to the eastward. They were broad on the port bow and drawing ahead of the convoy at high speed. It was now also seen that there were five destroyers present instead of the reported four. Commander Scurfield (in HMS Bedouin led out the ‘Fleet’ destroyers to attack while HMS Cairo and the remainder of the convoy escort started making smoke to cover the merchant ships, which were ordered to turn to starboard and to seek shelter in Tunisian waters. It was A/Capt. Hardy’s intention to gain as much time as possible to enable an air striking force from Malta to attack the enemy.

At 0640 B/15, the Italian cruisers opened fire at a range of over 20000 yards. Their second salvo straddled HMS Cairo and others fell near the convoy before the smoke screen could take effect. The British ships could not yet reply as the enemy was still out of range. As the ‘Fleet’ destroyers gathered way, they became strung out in a loose line of bearing, nearly line ahead, in the order HMS Bedouin, HMS Partridge, HMS Ithuriel, HMS Marne and HMS Matchless, though the last ship worked up to 32 knots in the endeavour to keep up. The first to destroyers opened fire on the enemy cruisers at 0645 B/15 with their guns at maximum elevation but in a quarter of an hour both Bedouin and Partridge were badly hit and stopped and the fight passed them by. Ithuriel held her fire till she got within 15000 yards, then she engaged a cruiser, which she eventually hit at a range of 8000 yards. Marne also engaged a cruiser, opening fire at over 18000 yards. In the meantime the Italian destroyers had fallen astern of the cruisers, three of them, in fact, soon left the line and disappeared to the northward. The last two enemy destroyers opened fire on the Marne from her port beam at around 0700 B/15 and she and Matchless, which was astern of her, replied. Both British destroyers soon found the range and hit one of the enemy (Ugolino Vivaldi) and drove them off. They then pressed on to engage the enemy cruisers which kept their distance and were zig-zagging and making smoke to upset the aim of the British ships.

As soon as the convoy was well behind the smoke screen and on it’s way to the westward. HMS Cairo and the four Hunt class escort destroyers were proceeding south and now also engaged the two enemy destroyers which had been engaged by Marne and Matchless. At about 0700 B/15 HMS Cairo came under fire from the enemy cruisers again. They were using two turrets each to engage the Cairo and two turrets to engage the ‘Fleet’ destroyers. HMS Cairo was hit by a 6” shell. She herself fired her 4” guns occasionally, though without much hope of doing real damage to the enemy.

At 0715 B/15, A/Capt. Hardy decided to concentrate the remaining three ‘Fleet’ destroyers on HMS Cairo and ordered HMS Ithuriel to join him. HMS Marne and HMS Matchless continued to engage the enemy for about half an hour. Though fire from both sides was accurate no hits were obtained on either side. At 0745 B/15 the Italians turned to port on which A/Capt. Hardy turned north and ordered all destroyers to join him.

Meanwhile, the convoy, 15 nautical miles away to the north-west, steering westwards, now turned to the south-east again. At 0705 B/15, now deprived of the support of HMS Cairo, all destroyers and escort destroyers, and without air support, the convoy was attacked by eight German JU 87 dive bombers. They sank the Chant and disabled the Kentucky. HMS Hebe took the Kentucky in tow. The convoy then went on until 0745 B/15 when course was changed to rejoin the escorts. The Italians however meanwhile where following the British escorts and kept them under fire.

At 0834 B/15, A/Capt. Hardy, ordered the convoy to reverse course while Cairo and the destroyers laid a smokescreen across it’s track. This seems to have baffled the Italians which first turned to the south-west and then at 0840 B/15 hauled round to the north-eastward and stood away. A/Capt. Hardy then sent the ‘Hunt’-class escort destroyers to rejoin the convoy and then led the ‘Fleet’ destroyers after the enemy. At this time HMS Cairo was hit for the second time. For the present however the Italians had given up the game. By 0930 B/15 they were out of sight and the British ships then turned to rejoin the convoy.

At 1030 B/15 the merchant vessel were back on their proper course to Malta, with the escort at full strength except for HMS Bedouin and HMS Partridge. Long-range Spitfires from Malta were patrolling overhead.

At 1040 B/15 a few German bombers appeared but these were driven off before they could drop their bombs. The fighters were able to shot one down. Unfortunately this exhausted fuel and ammunition of the Spitfires which were operating at their extreme range so when at 1120 B/15 another attack started they were not able to repel it. Their relief had not yet arrived.

It was a combination of high-level and dive bombing by Ju. 88’s and Ju. 87’s. Gunfire destroyed one of the German’s. One or two were shot down afterwards by the relieving Spitfires which had arrived during the attack. By then however the merchant vessel Burdwan was disabled. There was still 150 nautical miles to go, with the likelihood of further attacks from the air and with Italian ships nearby. A/Capt. Hardy therefore decided that he had no other choice then to sacrifice the damaged Kentucky and Burdwan as the best way to save the rest of the convoy whose speed would otherwise be reduced to six knots. He ordered HMS Hebe and HMS Badsworth to sink the cripples which enabled the remaining two merchant ships to continue at their best speed.

At 1315 B/15, dive-bombers attacked yet again. And again there was no fighter cover present over the convoy. This time however the German’s were unsuccessful. One bomber out of twelve was shot down by the ships AA fire while the relief flight of Spitfires came in time to shoot down two more as the enemy retired. This was the last time the convoy was attacked from the air before it arrived at Malta under the protection from short-range Spitfires. The next threat of attack came from the Italian warships which closed the convoy once more.

After the engagement in the morning the Italian cruisers had gone back to join up with their destroyers, one of wich had been badly damaged by HMS Marne and HMS Matchless. While preparing to take this destroyer in tow the Italians were disrupted by British aircraft. Malta had been able to sent a small torpedo aircraft force to attack them. Four Albacores followed by two Beauforts attacked them about 12 nautical miles south of Pantelleria at 1030 B/15. Unfortunately without success.

The two cruisers with two destroyers then went south again hoping to find stagglers from the convoy. They found HMS Hebe, which was on her way back to rejoin the convoy, having left the tanker Kentucky in a sinking condition astern. HMS Hebe sighted the enemy a long way to the north at 1255 B/15. In the next half an hour the enemy was able to close as to open fire on the small minesweeper and eventually she was hit.

On receiving Hebe’s enemy report, A/Capt. Hardy, left the convoy in HMS Cairo taking the three remaining ‘Fleet’ destroyers with him; HMS Ithuriel, HMS Marne and HMS Matchless. Besides the Hebe to protect there were other ships coming back from the scuttled merchantmen and also HMS Bedouin and HMS Partridge which, A/Capt. Hardy believed to be following the convoy.

At 1355 B/15 the Italians gave up the chase, presumably on sighting HMS Cairo and turned to engage a target to the westward. This could only be HMS Bedouin and HMS Partridge but A/Capt. Hardy felt bound to return to the convoy, then nearly 15 nautical miles off, though it meant leaving the damaged destroyers to their fate.

These two ships had been had been striving to preserve themselves for the King’s service ever since they had been crippled in the morning. HMS Partridge was ready to steam again by 0745 B/15, three-quarters of an hour after being put out of action. She prepared to take HMS Bedouin in tow as that ship was entirely disabled. These preparations were disrupted by two Italian destroyers which had to be driven away. By 1000 B/15 however Bedouin was being towed by Partridge and the two ships were proceeding slowly towards the convoy which they had orders to join. They met it at 1145 B/15. There was still hope to get one engine going in HMS Bedouin but later on it became evident that this hope had to be abandoned. It was then thought best to try to make it to Gibraltar.

At 1320 B/15, the Italian Squadron came into sight again and two destroyers were apparently closing the two British destroyers while there were also enemy dive-bombers flying around. HMS Partridge therefore had no choice then to slip the tow and to lay smoke around HMS Bedouin. As the enemy cruisers approached, after their chase of HMS Hebe, HMS Partridge stood away to draw their fire and in this she succeeded. She was straddled from long range at 1400 B/15. It was the intention the return to HMS Bedouin later but the latter ship was torpedoed by an Italian torpedo bomber at 1425 B/15 and she sank within a few minutes but not before shooting down the attacker. The enemy surface ships also sank the derelict Kentucky and Burdwan around the same time. Kentucky was finished off by the Oriani while Burdwan was possibly sunk by the Ascari.

A/Capt. Hardy rejoined the convoy at 1530 B/15 after the last encounter with the Italian squadron. At 1730 B/15, HMS Welshman rejoined the convoy south of Linosa coming from Malta. She had arrived there in the morning and was sent out again by Vice-Admiral Leatham as soon as she had landed her cargo.

Then at 1910 B/15, there was another air attack. Upon that time the enemy had been kept away by the strong fighter escort from Malta directed by the radar in HMS Cairo. Twelve German bombers managed to close and near misses were obtained on HMS Welshman, HMS Matchless and the merchant Troilus.
A last attempt was foiled at 2040 B/15 by the fighters from Malta and the ships guns. There was now only one danger to be overcome, enemy mines.
HMS Liverpool

At 1420 B/15, three torpedo aircraft made a final unsuccessful attempt to attack HMS Liverpool after which she, HMS Antelope and HMS Westcott were not again molested. That afternoon the tug HMRT Salvonia arrived from Gibraltar and they took over the tow. Antelope then joined Westcott as A/S screen. With Salvonia came also the A/S trawler HMS Lady Hogarth (T/Lt. S.G. Barnes, RNR).
'Force Y'.

At 2345 B/15 the Italian submarine Bronzo sighted an enemy escort vessel of the 'Kingfisher-class' which opened fire on the submarine in position 36°50'N, 00°10'E. This was HMS Coltsfoot. The submarine was depth-charged and escaped by going down to 117 metres.

It had been intended that the minesweepers would be ahead of the convoy when approaching Malta but owning to mistakes the convoy arrived first. The result was that one of the two remaining merchant vessels, the Orari, the destroyer HMS Matchless, two escort destroyers HMS Badsworth, ORP Kujawiak and the minesweeper HMS Hebe hit mines. Fortunately damage was light except for ORP Kujawiak which unfortunately sank in three minutes.
After having taken on board ammunition at Malta, HMS Cairo, HMS Ithuriel, HMS Marne, HMS Middleton and HMS Blankney departed the island in the evening to return to Gibraltar.

Shortly after 0800 B/16, the destroyer HMS Panther (Lt.Cdr. R.W. Jocelyn, RN) joined the A/S screen of the disabled HMS Liverpool. Two more vessels came out from Gibraltar to join the A/S screen, these were the corvette HMS Jonquil (Lt.Cdr. R.E.H. Partington, RD, RNR) which joined around 0940 B/16. At 1530 B/16, the motor launch ML 458 joined.

As HMS Cairo and the two destroyers and two escort destroyers were skirting along the African coast they were shadowed from sunrise onward. They were however not attacked until midday, when they were passed the Galita bank. From then until 2030 B/17 that evening, German bombers pestered them continuously. The Germans came sometimes in flights of six, though generally in flights of two and three. Main target seems to have been HMS Ithuriel which had a tough time and sustained some minor damage due to leaks from near misses. During the attacks one enemy bomber was shot down by HMS Cairo.

At 2017 B/17, they joined with Vice-Admiral Curteis with HMS Kenya and HMS Charybdis in position 37°30’N, 04°30’E. After leaving the convoy in the evening of the 14th, the Vice-Admiral had taken ‘Force W’ some 400 nautical miles to the west of Sardinia in order to avoid observation and attack while waiting for the return of ‘Force X’. His ships had however been shadowed on the 15th and was then attacked by two small groups of torpedo aircraft. Hurricanes from HMS Eagle forced them to drop their torpedoes from long range. They were also able to shoot down one of the attackers.

From the morning of the 16th to noon on the 17th, Vice-Admiral Curteis, cruised with HMS Kenya and HMS Charybdis near the rendez-vous position. HMS Malaya both aircraft carriers and the remaining destroyers had been sent to Gibraltar around 0800/16. They arrived at Gibraltar around 1030/17.

Around noon on the 17th, Vice-Admiral Curteis, with his two cruisers proceeded eastwards to meet up with A/Capt. Hardy’s force after which they proceeded in company to Gibraltar where they arrived in the early evening of the 18th.
HMS Liverpool and her escorts safely arrived at Gibraltar late in the afternoon of the 17th. (18)

13 Jun 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) fires four torpedoes at the Italian light cruisers Raimondo Montecuccoli and Emanuelle Filiberto Duca d'Aosta south of Sardinia, Italy in position 38°56'N, 09°40'E. The torpedoes however missed their targets. The Italian cruisers were escorted by the Italian destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Vincenzo Gioberti and Ascari.

1253 B/13 - In approximate position 38°53'N, 09°42'E, sighted masts and smoke bearing 015°. Came up to 20 feet. The vessel sighted appeared to be a cruiser of which only the masts and tops were visible. The vessel was steering a course of 270°.
1254 B/13 - Increased speed to intercept. Brought all tubes to the ready.
1319 B/13 - The 'cruiser' was identified as a small destroyer towing very close astern a white two-masted schooner with a large gaff. Broke off the attack and returned to the patrol position.
1548 B/13 - Put deep by two flying boats bearing 330° patrolling towards.
1813 B/13 - Sighted smoke. This was soon seen to come from an Italian force of two cruisers escorted by four destroyers. Also three aircraft were patrolling overhead. Started attack.
1831 B/13 - Fired four torpedoes.
1836 B/13 - Heard a loud explosion.
1838 B/13 - Heard two depth charge explosions followed by a 'swishing' noise. Went to 90 feet.
1842 B/13 - Picked up HE going slow on bearing 120°. Went to 120 feet.
1843 B/13 - HE and A/S transmissions which were sweeping passed up the starboard side. Went to 150 feet and noises blanked out as P 43 passed a heavier density layer at 125 feet.

2225 B/13 - Surfaced and passed an enemy report timed 1831 B/13 which contained the following; Two Italian Cruisers of the Eugenio di Savoia-class and four destroyers, course 135° speed 20 knots in position 38°56'N, 09°41'E. One hit on one cruiser. (19)

14 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 2252 B/14, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following information to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN); Two cruisers and four destroyers reported leaving Palermo at 2125 B/14. (20)

16 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1440 B/16, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN); Return route to Gibraltar. Route 'l', P 211 and P 43 through the following positions; F) 38°30'N, 09°00'E. G) 38°01'N, 01°59'E. H) 180° - Cape de Gata - 15 miles. Route '2', P 44 and P 42 through the following positions; J) 38°17'N, 10°01'E. K) 37°40'N, 01°59'E. L) 180° - Cape de Gata - 35 miles. Speed of advance, P 211 115 miles per day, U-class 100 miles per day. Leave patrol diving by day as follows; Route '1' passing position 'F', P 211 at 0800 B/17, P 43 at 0800 B/18. Route '2' passing position 'J', P 44 at 0800 B/17, P 42 at 0800 B/18. Arriving at Gibraltar P 211 at 0930 B/23, P 43 at 0930 B/25, P 44 at 1400 B/24 and P 42 at 1400 B/25. P 44 and P 42 proceed on surface on the last day of the passage. Bombing restrictions have same limit as outward route. (20)
17 Jun 1942 At 1845 B/17, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) set course to leave patrol in accordance with the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic's signal timed 1440 B/16. (21)
20 Jun 1942 At 1235 B/20, in approximate position 38°06'N, 04°46'E, the Italian repatriation ship Saturnia (23940 GRT, built 1927) passed 7 miles to the southwards.

The Saturnia and Vulcania (24648 GRT, built 1928) sailed from East Africa for the repatriation of Italian civilians. They reached Naples at 1312 B/21 and sailed again the following day for Leghorn and Genoa. (21)
21 Jun 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) attacked the Italian submarine Bronzo near position 38°01'N, 03°24'E with a full salvo of torpedoes. No hits were obtained.

The Italians had seen another submarine coming straight towards but were under orders not to attack other submarines. They had not seen any torpedo tracks but heard three explosions and presumed the submarine had been enemy which had fired torpedoes at her.
1900 B/21 - In approximate position 37°57'N, 03°35'E, sighted the Vichy-French merchant vessel Djebel Aures (2835 GRT, built 1929) steering 010° obviously coming from Algiers.
2217 B/21 - Surfaced.
2325 B/21 - Sighted a surfaced submarine bearing 195° at a range of 1.5 miles. Altered course towards and started attack.
2328 B/21 - Fired a full salvo of four torpedoes. No hits were obtained as the enemy submarine turned away. The torpedo tracks ran very close up the port side of the enemy.
2353 B/21 - The enemy submarine was sighted bearing 100° down moon. As the enemy appeared to have a considerable margin of speed Lt. Halliday decided to dive until the moon had set.
0215 B/22 - Surfaced and proceeded.

0255 B/22 - Cleared an enemy report and amanded the E.T.A. This signal was timed 2328 B/21 and contained the following; One U-boat attacked in position 38°01'N, 03°24'E, course 045°, speed 5 knots. No hits. E.T.A. Gibraltar when proceeding on surface on the last day at 1600 B/25. (16)
25 Jun 1942 At 1830 B/25, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar.
At Gibraltar the battery was removed and a new battery was installed. (16)
11 Jul 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) is docked in No.3 graving dock at the Gibraltar Dockyard. (22)
15 Jul 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) is undocked. (22)
17 Jul 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Gibraltar together with HMS Vansittart (Lt.Cdr. T. Johnston, RN). (22)
22 Jul 1942 Around 1830 B/22, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Gibraltar for Malta.
She is to pass through position 36°07'N, 01°52'W at 0300 B/24 and 36°44'N, 00°05'E at 0300 B/25, thence on course 080°.

24 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 1329 B/24 the Vice-Admiral Malta sent the following orders to HMS Clyde (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); Proceed through positions 'C' and 'D' and adjust speed to arrive in the following positions at sunset, Clyde in 36°21'N, 13°49'N on 28 July and P 43 36°43'N, 13°32'E on 31 July. Further instructions will be signalled on conclusion channel sweeping now progressing. You will be expected to surface off Grand Harbour at dusk, Clyde on 29 July, P 43 on 1 August.

In a follow up signal timed 1125 B/26, the Vice-Admiral Malta set the following orders to these two submarines; Cancel the last sentence in my 1329 B/24. Proceed in accordance with the Capt.S.10's signal timed 1847 B/26 [we have been unable to find the text of this signal] to arrive off Marsamxett as requisite, Clyde on 29 July, P 43 on 1 August. (23)
31 Jul 1942 At 1330 B/31, in position 37°33'N, 12°33'E, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) a grey two-masted schooner of about 100 tons steering 355° at 5 knots under an auxiliary motor.

At 1415 B/31, two miles further to the east a similarly-built schooner, painted black, with a French flag on a staff at the stern. This schooner was proceeding on a similar course as the previous schooner sighted. (3)
2 Aug 1942 Around 1215-1230 B/2, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta while HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived at Malta from Gibraltar.
They were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 1000 B/2. (24)
12 Aug 1942 Around 1730 B/12, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the west coast of Greece.
She was swept out by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which departed Malta at the same time to lay navigational marks. (3)
12 Aug 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 43 during her 4th war patrol see the map below.

14 Aug 1942 At 2000 B/14, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) passed the 19th meridian, but remained on the surface as it was hoped to reach the position of the aircraft attack of Zante that had been mentioned in the Senior Officer Submarines Alexandria's (S.S.O.X.) signal timed 1415 C/14 which contained the following; My 1232 C/14th. 1) Consider ultimate destination probably Benghazi. Between 0200 C/15 and 0401 C/15 aircraft will search east and west of Sante and attack using flares. 2) May be for Tobruk but Benghazi more likely. A.S.V. aircraft will search, report and drop flares between 0100 C/15 and 0300 C/15.

This signal referred to a signal timed 1232 C/15. This signal contained the following; 1) At 1145 C/14, position, course and speed of two large merchant ships and four destroyers, reported by aircraft. 39°25'N, 18°30'E, 120°, 13 knots. 2) At 1130 C/14, aircraft report one merchant ship and two destroyers, position, course and speed, 36°14'N, 23°36'E, 200°, 15 knots.

The convoy reported at 1145 C/14 was made up of the transport Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the Italian destroyer Nicoloso da Recco and the torpedo boat Polluce who had left Taranto at 0225-0355 B/14 and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the torpedo boats Calliope and Castore who sailed from Brindisi at 0429 B/14. Both convoys merged at 1010-1027 B/14. They were bound for Benghazi.

At 1829 B/15, Lerici was torpedoed by HMS Porpoise in position 34°50'N, 21°30'E or 34°44'N, 21°35'E (English vs Italian sources). Ravello escorted by Da Recco and Castore proceeded on and arrived at Benghazi at 0830-0940 B/16. They left behind Calliope and Polluce to respectively pick up the survivors and hunt the submarine. Polluce dropped 38 depth charges in three runs and claimed the submarine sunk but HMS Porpoise had escaped. The destroyers Mitragliere and Bersagliere sailed from Navarino at 0110 B/16 to help tow Lerici and joined at 0825 B/16 but the transport was heavily on fire and this proved to be impossible. The derelict Lerici was finished off with 89 rounds of 120mm/50 from the destroyer Mitragliere at 1102 B/16.

The convoy reported at 1130 C/14 was made up of the transport Foscolo (Italian, 4538 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian destroyer Saetta and the Italian torpedo boats Orione and Sirio. They had sailed from Piraeus at 0215-0300 B/14 (except Saetta who joined from Suda (sailed at 0336 B/14) at 0825 B/14). Sirio was detached at 1500 B/14 and reached Heraklion at 0430 B/15. The convoy arrived at Benghazi at 1035 B/15. (25)

15 Aug 1942 At 0240 B/15, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) reached the last ordered position, 37°40'N, 20°01'E. She then proceeded eastwards along the southern limit of her patrol area with the object of seeing the aircraft flares which would be dropped during their attacks.

At 0822 B/15, Cape Marathia (Zante) was sighted bearing 065° at a range of 15 miles. Nothing had been sighted of a possible air attack. P 43 then proceeded northwards along the suspected fast convoy route about 15 miles to the west of Zante but nothing was sighted. (3)
16 Aug 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) attacked an escorted Italian merchant vessel with four torpedoes west of Lefkada Island, Greece. No hits were obtained.

This was the Italian transport Chisone (6168 GRT, built 1922) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Generale Antonino Cascino. Three torpedo tracks were sighted and she turned in time to see them passing a short distance ahead. Cascino dropped a few depth charges and was joined by an escorting aircraft which dropped a couple of bombs.

The convoy sailed had from Brindisi at 1850 B/15. At 1427 B/16, three torpedo wakes were sighted on the starboard side. They missed Chisone ahead, and a fourth was observed to have missed her astern (the latter was not seen by the escorting torpedo boat). Generale Antonino Cascino dropped a few depth charges assisted by escorting aircraft but did observe any result. They arrived in Patras at 2200 B/16.
1350 B/16 - Sighted an aircraft flying south, searching.

1358 B/16 - Sighted the masts of a vessel to the north. Started attack on a partly laden southbound merchant vessel. She was escorted by an older type Italian torpedo boat. Also one of possibly two aircraft were patrolling the area.

1424 B/16 - In position 38°42'N, 20°31'E fired four torpedoes from 2000 yards. One torpedo caused a splash on firing and this was most likely seen by the enemy. No hits were obtained. Went deep on firing.
1432 B/16 - The torpedo boat started a counter attack.
1446 B/16 - The last of 9 depth charges was dropped. Only minor damage had been caused to P 43.
1740 B/16 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. (3)

16 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1020 C/16, the Senior Officer, Submarines, Alexandria (S.S.O.X.), informed HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) of the following; At 0930/16, aircraft reported two large merchant ships and three destroyers in position 32°23'N, 20°02'E, course 035°, speed 14 knots.

This referred to a convoy made up of the Italian transports Nino Bixio (7137 GRT, built 1941) and Sestriere (7992 GRT, built 1942) which had just left Benghazi escorted by the Italian destroyers Nicoloso da Recco, Saetta and the Italian torpedo boats Castore and Orione.

This convoy had sailed from Benghazi at 1340 B/16-1518 B/16. At 1535 B/17, in position 36°36'N, 21°30'E, Nino Bixio was hit by two torpedoes from HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSC, RN). There were 434 victims, including 118 PoW's. Saetta picked up the survivors and took the stricken vessel in tow while Orione hunted the submarine. At 2010 B/17, the torpedo boat Polluce sailed from Navarino to briefly escort her, then proceeded to Kalamata where she arrived at 0245 B/18. Nino Bixio towed by Saetta arrived in Navarino at 0717 B/18. Sestriere proceeded escorted by Nicoloso da Recco and Castore. At 0822 B/18, Nicoloso da Recco signalled a submarine on the port side. Castore was left behind to hunt the submarine. The convoy went on and arrived in Brindisi at 1700 B/18-1732 B/18. At 0856 B/18, in position 39°30'N, 18°58'E, Castore dropped a first pattern of depth charges, followed by three more at 0902 B/11, 0911 B/11 and 0936 B/18. At 1120 B/18, she was short on fuel and left the area. She reached Brindisi at 1732 B/18. (26)

17 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1211 C/17, the Senior Officer, Submarines, Alexandria (S.S.O.X.) informed HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) of the following; At 1115 C/17, aircraft reported one merchant ship and two destroyers in position 39°40'N, 18°25'E, course 120°, speed 14 knots.

This was the Italian transport Unione (6071 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boat Antares They had sailed from Taranto at 0253 B/17-0340 B/17. At 1715 B/17, the escort was reinforced by the Italian torpedo boat Calliope, which had sailed from Patras at 0755 B/17 to carry out an A/S sweep ahead of the convoy. They arrived in Patras at 0100 B/18-0220 B/18. The convoy sailed from Patras (minus Antares which proceeded to Corfu and Calliope which remained in Patras) at 0555 B/21 through the Corinth Canal and arrived in Piraeus at 1748 B/21. Unione and Folgore sailed from Piraeus 0042 B/25. They were reinforced by the destroyer Saetta, which sailed from Navarino at 0606 B/27, and they arrived in Benghazi at 1500 B/28-1527 B/28.

In a signal timed 1531 C/17, the S.S.O.X. informed HMS P 43 of the following; Air reconnaissance shows the following; 1) Two merchant ships and four destroyers in position 38°05'N, 20°15'E, course 325°, speed 14 knots at 1312 C/17. 2) One merchant ship and two destroyers in position 35°26'N, 22°32'E, course 320°, speed 13 knots at 1425 C/17.

Sighting 1) referred to the same convoy that had been sighted leaving Benghazi the day before, the Italian transports Nino Bixio (7137 GRT, built 1941) and Sestriere (7992 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian destroyers Nicoloso da Recco, Saetta and the Italian torpedo boats Castore and Orione [See also the 'event' for 16 August 1942.]

Sighting 2) referred to the German transport Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the German destroyer ZG 3 / Hermes and the Italian torpedo boat Partenope sailed from Tobruk at 1815 B/16-1830 B/16. They arrived at Kalamata at 0100 B/18-0245 B/18. At 0800 B/17, the Italian destroyer Premuda sailed from Suda and relieved ZG 3 / Hermes at 1515 B/17. The German destroyer arrived in Piraeus at 0145 B/18. At 0600 B/18, Ankara escorted by Premuda and the Italian torpedo boats Polluce and Partenope sailed from Kalamata. They were briefly reinforced by the Italian torpedo boat Calliope (from 2140 B/18 to 2340 B/18). Ankara, Premuda, Partenope and Polluce arrived in Brindisi at 1350 B/19-1425 B/19. (26)

19 Aug 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel with four torpedoes to the north-west of Lefkada Island, Greece. No hits were obtained. This was the German transport Balkan (3838 GRT, built 1914). Three torpedo tracks were observed. She was escorted by an Italian auxiliary which dropped a few depth charges.

Balkan had sailed unescorted from Dubrovnik at 0600 B/15 via Durazzo (2000 B/15-1030 B/16) and Valona (1800 B/16-0630 B/19) for Patras, the last leg escorted by a small [so far unidentified] Italian auxiliary. They arrived in Patras at 0810 B/20.
1850 B/19 - Sighted smoke to the northward.

1856 B/19 - Sighted masts of a vessel bearing 007°. Started attack on what soon turned out to be a deep laden southbound merchant vessel of about 5000 tons. She was escorted by what is thought to be an armed yacht and one aircraft.
1950 B/19 - In position 38°57'N, 20°24'E fired four torpedoes from 2200 yards. P 43 went to 150 feet and took avoiding action.
1952 B/19 - A loud explosion was heard at the correct running range. HE of the target was not heard afterwards.
1956 B/19 - A counter attack was started. Six depth charges were dropped but none was very close.
2045 B/19 - HE of the escort faded out to the east.
2400 B/19 - Surfaced. Nothing in sight. (3)

19 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1146 B/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); Leave position 38°24'N, 19°45'E P.M./21 and proceed through positions 35°22'N, 15°13'E, 35°22'N, 14°12'E and 35°32'N, 14°12'E to arrive at the end of the searched channel at 1001 B/25 approaching from the south-east. HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, DSC, RN) is expected at the same time. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) leaves position 35°39'N, 15°49'E at dusk/21 direct for position 37°47'N, 19°59'E and may proceed on the surface during daylight between 17°00'E and 19°00'E.
In a signal timed 2240 B/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 43; Leave patrol as in my 1146 C/19 P.M./22 to arrive A.M./26. (26)
20 Aug 1942 At 0406 B/20, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) cleared her signal timed 0243 B/20 in which she reported that all torpedoes were expended.
At 1220 B/20, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0943 B/20 was received which ordered HMS P 43 to return to Malta to arrive at 1001 B/24 by the route previously ordered.
At 2100 B/20, HMS P 43 left patrol to return to Malta. (25)
24 Aug 1942 Around 1230 B/24, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was swept in by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) which had joined around 1010 B/24. (3)

3 Sep 1942 At 1530 B/3, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Ionian Sea to the south-west of Peloponnesos.
She departed Malta together with HMS Clyde (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN). The submarines were escorted out until 1815 B/3 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN).
P 43 was to proceed through positions 35°29'N, 15°07'E at 2030 B/4, 35°47'N, 17°43'E at 2030 B/5, diving by day, for position 36°15'N, 20°42'E. (27)
3 Sep 1942 For the daily positions of HMS P 43 during her 5th war patrol see the map below.

4 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1639 B/4, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); P 34 proceed direct to arrive in position 37°55'N, 19°57'E by 0301 B/6 thence to centre of area ?Z [????]. P 43 proceed direct to arrive in position 36°06'N, 20°48'E by 2259 B/6. Passage to be made on the surface during daylight as necessary to comply. Further instructions will be signalled by Capt. S.1. (28)

5 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1525 B/5, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); Air reconnaissance shows a submerged enemy submarine in position 34°38'N, 19°21'E at 1435 B/5, course about 351°. Anticipate position 2100 B/5, 35°27'N, 19°11'E and crosses P 43's line of advance about 0001 B/6.
This must have referred to the Italian submarine Atropo which had sailed from Benghazi at 1530 B/4 and arrived in Taranto at 1110 B/7. (29)

5 Sep 1942 At 2124 B/5, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1710 C/5, which ordered her to patrol in the north-west corner of area 'K' in position 36°15'N, 20°44'E.
At 2125 B/5, the glare of lights were seen on bearing 010°. P 43 turned towards to investigate.

At 2236 B/5, the range was now approximately 8 miles and it was not possible to close any further. This was presumed to be a hospital ship. Position was 36°00'N, 18°20'E. Course of the vessel was estimated to be 300° at 12 knots.
This was the Italian Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) which had sailed from Tobruk at 1800 B/3 and stopped at Derna 1200 B/4-1900 B/4 and arrived in Naples at 0730 B/7.

At 2350 B/5, a position 12 miles ahead of the estimated 0030 B/6 position of the enemy submarine reported in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1525 B/5. P 43 turned bow on to the expected track of the enemy and listening watch was set while proceeding at slow speed.
At 0208 B/6, HMS P 43 continued her passage to her patrol position as nothing had been seen or heard. (27)

6 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1850 C/6, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders; Proceed forthwith diving by day to following positions; HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), 35°48'N, 21°37'E HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), 35°15'N, 21°00'E HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), 35°05'N, 21°51'E HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN) 33°33'N, 21°42'N HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, DSO, RN) only to be in position 32°31'N, 23°34'E by dawn September 8th.

In a signal timed 2047 B/6, they were informed that three merchant ships and ten destroyers had been reported at 1740 B/6 in position 38°46'N, 20°13'E proceeding on course 170° at 10 knots. They were also informed that aircraft may attack the enemy convoy north of Navarino tonight.

In a signal timed 2115 C/6, further instructions were sent by Capt. S.1. HMS P 34, HMS P 43 and HMS Una move to intercept from positions ordered in my 1850 C/6 as follows; HMS P 34, 318° - 40 miles. HMS P 43, 024° - 32 miles. HMS Una, 250° - 18 miles. Be in new positions by dawn 7th Sept. HMS Traveller, ignore my 1850 C/6 and be in position 32°45'N, 20°35'E by 0001 C/8.

These submarines were to operate against an Italian convoy operation; At 0036 B/6-0150 B/6, convoy 'N', consisting of the transports Sestriere (Italian 7991 GRT, built 1942) and Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere (escort leader), Lampo, Legionario (equipped with new German DETE Gerät) and the Italian torpedo boats Pegaso and Partenope sailed from Brindisi.

At 0012 B/6-0205 B/6, convoy 'P', consisting of transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Manara (Italian 7124 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the italian destroyers Fuciliere (escort leader), Bombardiere, Corsaro, Freccia, Camicia Nera, Geniere and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade sailed from Taranto.

The two convoys were to merge for defensive purposes, but then to split, this time the three Italian ships proceeding to Benghazi and Ankara to Tobruk. At 0100 B/6, the torpedo boat Procione sailed from Piraeus to reinforce the convoy. At 1040 B/6, the two convoys merged and the destroyer Aviere assumed overall command. The combined convoy was now known as convoy 'L'.

At 1525 B/6, Procione joined convoy and was thus present during air attack when Manara was torpedoed at 1530 B/6. The air attack was carried out by fourteen to sixteen torpedo bombers, Manara was hit in the stern section and taken in tow by Freccia and escorted by Legionario and Procione. They proceeded to Corfu. At 1855 B/6, Legionario left them to rejoin the convoy. At 1003 B/7, Manara was brought to the Bay of Arilla (near Cape Cavobianco, Corfu).

At 1810 B/6, the convoy split. Ankara escorted by the destroyers Fuciliere (which resumed duty as escort leader), Geniere, Lampo, Bombardiere and the torpedo boat Partenope slowed down at 10 knots to distance itself from Ravello and Sestriere escorted by the destroyers Aviere, Legionario, Corsaro, Camicia Nera and the torpedo boats Pallade and Pegaso, which forged ahead. The Ankara section was now known as Convoy 'P', the Ravello section as Convoy 'N'.
At 0449 B/7, Legionario rejoined the Ankara convoy.

At 0547 B/7, the Ankara convoy sighted the Ravello/Sestriere convoy and at 0737 B/7, the two convoys merged again and were now known, once again as convoy 'L', with Aviere once more assuming overall command.

At 0927 B/7, Lampo reported torpedo tracks in position 36°10'N, 21°05'E (this was the attack by HMS P 34) and Sestriere signalled she had avoided torpedoes from the port side by turning to starboard. Lampo was ordered to hunt the submarine. At 1355 B/7, she signalled that the A/S search had not yielded result and that she expected to rejoin the convoy at at about 1900 B/7. She caught up at 1738 B/7.
At 1810 B/7, three enemy aircraft attacked the combined convoy which opened up with antiaircraft fire. Bombs fell near Ankara.

At 1941 B/7, the convoy split again, Fuciliere assuming command of convoy 'P' which proceeded to Tobruk. Lampo had been assigned to join the escort of the Benghazi convoy, but as it had been reduced to two ships, Fuciliere requested from Aviere that she reinforce the Ankara convoy and this was granted.
Convoy 'N' was now formed of Ravello and Sestriere escorted by Aviere, Corsaro, Legionario, Camicia Nera, Pegaso and Pallade.

At 0216 B/8, in position 33°30'N, 22°43'E, a low-flying aircraft dropped two or three bombs which narrowly missed Fuciliere (2 metres!) causing massive damage, the shock disabled her gyro compass and her radio but she managed to make 12 knots and remained with the convoy. At 0302 B/8, Fuciliere, by visual signal, ordered Geniere to take over command of the convoy. At 0353 B/8, Fuciliere left the convoy, to make for Crete. Partenope had also turned north to escort her but Fuciliere ordered her to rejoin the convoy. At 0850 B/8, Bombardiere informed Fuciliere that she had been ordered by Geniere to assist her. Fuciliere replied that she was in urgent need of a towing. Bombardiere was proceeding at 25 knots but had trouble locating Fuciliere as the latter could only give a very approximate position due to the breakdown of her gyrocompass. She finally found her at 1830 B/8 near Sphakia and at 2355 B/8 had taken the stricken vessel in tow. At 0729 B/9, Geniere rejoined them. At 0849 B/9, they dropped anchor at Castel Selino (SE Crete) and at 1230 B/9, the Italian destroyer Nicolò Zeno arrived to assist them. At 1810 B/9, the four destroyers left for Navarino where they arrived at 0905 B/10-0919 B/10.

In the meantime, at 0636 B/8, the Ankara convoy was joined by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Brioni (1987 GRT, built 1931) escorted by the torpedo boat Orione which had sailed from Suda at 1330 B/7. Ankara, Brioni, Geniere, Lampo, Partenope and Orione arrived at Tobruk at 1315-1428 B/8. Geniere sailed at 1701 B/8 for Taranto but was later diverted to assist Fuciliere (see above).

Convoy 'N' arrived at Benghazi at 1021 B/8-1321 B/8. Ravallo, Sestriere, Legionario and Pallade entered harbour while Aviere, Corsaro and Camicia Nera remained outside, awaiting instructions from Supermarina, while Pegaso was detached to assist Fuciliere but eventualle Pegaso was recalled and entered Benghazi at 2000 B/8. (29)
7 Sep 1942 At 0525 B/7, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) dived in the ordered patrol position.
At 0821 B/7, the first of a series of explosions, over 100 in all at intervals, all day, at varying distances. None of these explosions were close.

At 1110 B/7, traces of smoke were seen bearing 277°. P 43 commenced to run in deep at 6 knots on course 230°. Lt. Halliday assumed the convoy proceeded at 10 knots on course 170° as had been signalled in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2047 B/6.

At 1148 B/7, on coming to periscope depth, four destroyers were sighted on bearing 350° at a range of 7000 yards. Two aircraft were sweeping ahead of these destroyers. This was presumed to be an A/S sweep ahead of the convoy. Course was altered to 360° to pass underneath these destroyers but there was no sign of the convoy behind.
At 1200 B/7, HMS P 43 was in position 34°43'N, 21°12'E. Two destroyers passed close either side of the submarine.
At 1210 B/7, one destroyer, which seemed to have turned back at 1155 B/7, passed very close doing 220 revs.

At 1215 B/7, P 43 came to periscope depth. There was no sign of the convoy. Four destroyers were seen on bearing 170° at a range of 6000 yards. One destroyer was thought to be of the Maestrale-class but the others could not be identified as they were stern on. No smoke was seen to the west so course was altered to 170° assuming the convoy would follow in the wake of the destroyers.

At 2017 B/7, P 43 surfaced. Nothing further having been seen. Set course to 195° to get on the track of the convoy as reported in Capt. S.10's signal timed 1514 B/7 with the intention of following up.

At 2107 B/7, course was altered to 090° to patrol off the Anti-Kithera channel as ordered in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1837 C/7. [See one of the other events listed for 7 September 1942 for the text of this signal.] (3)

7 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 0956 C/7, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, DSO, RN), HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN). On completion of attacks submarines should if possible follow up convoys to finish off damaged ships. This should be a standard practice, A/S measures permitting.
In a follow up signal timed 1015 C/7, these submarines were informed that Allied aircraft had been ordered not to attack submarines unless positively identified as enemy in areas 'K', 'B' and 'T'.

In a signal timed 1514 B/7 and amended in a signal timed 1619 B/7, these submarines were informed by Capt. S.10 that three transports and nine destroyers had been sighted at 1450 B/7 in position 35°30'N, 21°40'N, on course 170° at 10 knots.
In a signal timed 2001 B/7, the Capt. S.10 informed these submarines that aircraft from Malta hope to attack the enemy convoy about midnight tonight. (29)

7 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1837 C/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN). If nothing has been sighted by dark proceed patrol area 'K' in sectors from position (A) 35°42'N, 23°28'E as follows: HMS Una, 170° to 215°. HMS P 43, 215° to 235°. P 34, 235° to 270°. Except when in pursuit of enemy submarines should keep to their sector, P 34 and Una not to approach within 5 miles of position (A) and P 43 within 10 miles of position (A). Attention drawn to my signal timed 1015/7th [see above]. (29)

9 Sep 1942 At 2123 B/9, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1940 C/9 which contained the following orders for P 43; Proceed as necessary to be within 20 miles of Suda Bay by noon/10. (3)
10 Sep 1942 At 0100 B/10, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) passed through the Anti-Kithera Channel.

At 0435 B/10, P 43 was now within 20 miles from Suda Bay. She then dived and proceeded south to make landfall, then to the north-east to establish patrol in the vicinity of position 35°45'N, 24°00'E to cover the western approach to Suda Bay.
At 2018 B/10, HMS P 43 surfaced. No vessel having been seen. Set course to proceed east and then south to patrol in the eastern approaches to Suda Bay. (3)
11 Sep 1942 At 0422 B/11, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) dived and set course to investigate Rethimnon harbour.
At 1145 B/11, HMS P 43 was in position 010° - Rethimnon Citadel - 7000 yards. No vessels were seen at anchor. Several caiques were seen inside the harbour.

At 1151 B/11, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1002 C/11 was received which contained the following orders; Leave patrol P.M. today, pass through Anti-Kithera Channel and position 35°20'N, 19°01'E thence as ordered by the Capt. S.10. (27)

12 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1046 B/12, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); Proceed through position 35°22'N, 14°12'E to rendezvous with the minesweeper at 1059 B/17 at the end of the searched channel. (29)
17 Sep 1942 At 1400 B/17, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) which had joined around 1045 B/17. (30)
