29 Sep 1942 At 1400 B/29, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the southern Adriatic.

She was escorted out until 1715 B/29 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN). Before parting company with the minesweeper two practice attacks were carried out during which the minesweeper acted as target. (3)
29 Sep 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 43 during her 6th war patrol see the map below.

1 Oct 1942 At 1800 B/1, on board HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), Lt. J.E.E.D. Haward, RN was diagnosed with sandfly fever. Half an hour later, Lt. Halliday decided to return to Malta to land this very sick officer. Early the next morning a rating was also diagnosed with sandfly fever. (3)
3 Oct 1942 At 1340 B/3, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived back at Malta where the two sick crewmembers were landed.
She, and HMS Parthian (Lt.Cdr. D. St. Clair-Ford, RN), were escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 1100 B/3. (3)
4 Oct 1942 Around 1500 A/4, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Malta to resume her patrol. She was however given a new area to patrol, off the west coast of Greece, near Navarino.
She left Malta together with HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN) which was to proceed to Alexandria.
The submarines were swept out, most likely by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR). The minesweeper parted company around 1630 A/4. (3)
6 Oct 1942 At 1848 A/6, on surfacing, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) sighted a glare of light bearing 030°. P 43 increased to full speed to intercept.

At 2008 A/6, in position 36°21'N, 18°05'E, the ship was seen to be a very large hospital ship. The enemy vessel was correctly illuminated and proceeding on course 320° at 20 knots. HMS P 43 resumed her passage to her patrol area.

This was the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) sailed from Benghazi at 1000 B/4, and arrived in Derna at 0900 B/5 (I assume to pick up casualties from the El Alamein front). She sailed from Derna at 1730 B/5 and arrived in Napoli at 0730 B/8. (3)
7 Oct 1942 At 2100 A/7, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived in her patrol area. She then proceeded to close Strovathi (Stamfani) Island. (3)
8 Oct 1942 At 1122 A/8, to the east of Strovathi (Stamfani) Island, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat passing at a range of 7000 yards on course 320° at 20 knots.
[We have been unable to identify this torpedo boat, none should have been in the immediate area.]
At 1929 A/8, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1133 A/8 was received which contained the following orders; Patrol between Strovathi (Stamfani) Island and Proti Island until further orders. (31)

10 Oct 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Enrichetta (4652 GRT, built 1907) about 12 nautical miles west-south-west of Kyparissia, Greece in position 37°11'N, 21°26'E. Enrichetta was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Angelo Bassini and they were en-route from Brindisi to Navarino. From a crew of forty-seven and 104 passengers, sixty-three were killed or missing.
They had sailed from Brindisi at 1715 B/8. After the sinking, Angelo Bassini arrived in Navarino at 1508 B/10.
1217 A/10 - In heavy rain sighted a vessel to the northward.
1218 A/10 - The vessel was seen to be a 5000 ton merchant vessel. Range was 1600 yards. Started attack.
1224 A/10 - In position 37°11'N, 21°26'E fired three torpedoes from 600 yards. All hit the target.
1225 A/10 - The ship was seen to be sinking. Due to the bad visibility the escort was not seen. P 43 retired to the south and later to the west at 120 feet.
1335 A/10 - Weather had improved and upon returning to periscope depth an older type Italian torpedo boat was sighted at a range of 5000 yards. P 43 went to 180 feet and continued to clear the area.
1645 A/10 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
2028 A/10 - Surfaced to charge to the south of Strovathi (Stamfani) Island.
2245 A/10 - Sighted a hospital ship to the north-west coming towards.

The hospital ship was later seen to be correctly illuminated but it was steering irregular courses at slow speed. It was in sight until 0415 A/11 when HMS P 43 dived. It is thought the vessel was awaiting daylight to enter Navarino.
This hospital ship was most likely the Italian Sicilia (9646 GRT, built 1924) which sailed at 1000 B/9 from Bari and arrived in San Nicolò (Aghios Nikolaos, Crete) at 1030 B/12. (3)

11 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1155 A/11, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); Leave patrol as necessary and proceed through positions 36°32'N, 20°01'E and 35°22'N, 15°05'E to surface 1001 A/16 at the end of the Malta searched channel.
In a signal timed 1815 A/11 the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 43; Patrol about 5 miles south-west of Sapienza Island until necessary to comply with my signal timed 1125 A/11. (32)
12 Oct 1942 At 1857 A/12, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) surfaced after a day of submerged patrol to the south-west of Sapienza Island. She then left patrol to return to Malta. (3)
13 Oct 1942 At 1546 A/13, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) sighted two aircraft sweeping 8 miles to the eastward.

At 1600 A/13, in the Ionian Sea in approximate position 36°19'N, 19°04'E, a small vessel was seen, probably a destroyer or torpedo boat which was apparently proceeding to the southward. Range was about 5 miles. HMS P 43 went to 150 feet.

This was most probably an escort of the Italian transport D’Annunzio (4537 GRT, built 1942) which sailed from Brindisi at 1850 B/12-1925 B/12, escorted by the Italian destroyers Nicoloso da Recco and Folgore, the Italian torpedo boats Clio and Ardito. At 0630 B/13, they joined up with the Italian transport Foscolo (4500 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian destroyer Lampo and the torpedo boat Italian Partenope which had sailed from Corfu at 2340 B/12-2355 B/12. They were attacked by enemy bombers at 2356 B/13, two small bombs missed Folgore, one by only 5 metres causing minor damage and five slightly wounded. They arrived in Benghazi at 1227 B/14-1350 B/14.
At 1715 A/13, distant explosions were heard.
At 1730 A/13, HMS P 43 returned to periscope depth. (3)
16 Oct 1942 Around 1445 A/16, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.

She was escorted in byHMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 1000 A/16. However during enemy air activity HMS P 43 dived shortly afterwards remaining submerged until around 1130 A/16. (3)

1 Nov 1942 Around 1025 A/1, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Sicily to provide cover for Operation Torch, the Allied landings in North Africa.
She, and HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), were swept out until around 1730 A/1 by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR). (3)
1 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 43 during her 7th war patrol see the map below.
2 Nov 1942 At 0434 A/2, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) dived to 120 feet in position 37°17'N, 12°56'E for the westbound passage through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area).
At 2116 A/2, HMS P 43 surfaced to the south of Marettimo Island in position 37°45'N, 12°05'E. P 43 then cleared the area to the west at full speed to make a situation report.
At 0147 A/3, HMS P 43 cleared her signal timed 0053 A/3. [we have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (3)
4 Nov 1942 At 0111 A/4, west-north-west of Ustica, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) encountered a small motor craft laying stopped ahead. P 43 dived to avoid being sighted.
At 0212 A/4, HE faded to the south. P 43 surfaced and proceeded. (3)

5 Nov 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) attacked a surfaced Italian submarine off Stromboli with four torpedoes. All missed. This was Acciaio proceeding from Augusta to Cagliari. She observed three torpedo tracks.

There are two possibilities for the hospital ship; The Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) sailed from Tobruk at 1655 B/3 and arrived in Naples at 0712 B/6. The Italian Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) sailed from Mersa Matruh at 1630 B/2 and arrived in Naples at 0730 B/6. The course does not make sense unless, for some reason, one of them briefly turned back.?
0350 A/5 - Rounded Stromboli Island.
0500 A/5 - Dived and commenced to patrol in position 'D' (165° - Stromboli - 5 miles).
0600 A/5 - The sea was glassy calm. Several floatplanes were in sight. Lt. Halliday decided to patrol at 90 feet with the Asdic watch closed up only coming to periscope depth for routines.

Between 1117 A/5 and 1207 A/5 explosions were heard coming from the direction of Messina. Nothing was seen and at 1207 A/5, HMS P 43 resumed periscope depth patrol as the water surface was now ruffled.
1620 A/5 - Steered eastwards to close masts and smoke sighted on bearing 124°. Started attack.
1654 A/5 - Identified the vessel as a large two-funnelled hospital ship in position 38°40'N, 15°26'E, steering a course of 135° at 16 knots. Broke off the attack.
1658 A/5 - Sighted the conning tower of a submarine steering to pass to the north. Started attack. Shortly afterwards the submarine was seen to be flying the Italian flag.
1710 A/5 - In position 38°43'N, 15°21'E fired four torpedoes from 2400 yards. All missed.
1712 A/5 - When no explosions were heard raised the periscope and saw that the target had turned away and most likely had sighted the tracks which were plainly visible.

1715 A/5 - The hospital ship was now bearing 010°, range one mile. One small merchant vessel and an unidentified escort vessel were seen bearing 070° at a range of 3 miles. All three vessels in sight were on course 135°. Dusk was setting in. P 43 went to 120 feet and withdrew to the north-east to reload.
1750 A/5 - HE of a small reciprocating engine making 170 revs. was heard to port approaching on bearing 280°. This vessel, thought to be a trawler, passed astern.
1758 A/5 - A sharp light explosion occurred fairly close.
1800 A/5 - The HE re-appeared from astern, came up the starboard side, grew very loud and again passed astern.
1823 A/5 - The HE was now fading on the port quarter and was subsequently lost in P 43's own wake.
2050 A/5 - Completed reloading the torpedo tubes. Surfaced. (3)
7 Nov 1942 At 0001 A/7, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) commenced shifting to position 'C' (38°27'N, 15°31'E) to exchange patrol positions with HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN).
At 0500 A/7, HMS P 43 dived an commenced periscope patrol in the vicinity of position 'C'. Enemy flying boats were patrolling in the vicinity most of the day.
At 0928 A/7, two small vessels were sighted bearing 125°.
At 0940 A/7, one of the small vessels was seen to look like a tug but she was not towing anything. The other vessel looked like a motor torpedo boat.
At 0944 A/7, Mushy HE was heard from the direction of the largest vessel (the 'tug'). The vessels were now pointing towards.
At 0945 A/7, HMS P 43 went to 170 feet and cleared the area to the north as the A/S conditions in the area were very favourable for the enemy.

At 1150 A/7, while in the process of returning to periscope depth for the 1200 hour W/T routine a loud rushing noise was heard for a few seconds along the port side. It was clearly audible inside the submarine which quickly returned to 120 feet.
At 1240 A/7, HMS P 43 returned to periscope depth. All was found to be clear and she returned to the vicinity of position 'C'. (3)
9 Nov 1942 At 0001 A/9, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) cleared position 'C' to return to position 'D' and thus exchange positions with HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN). (3)

10 Nov 1942 At 1534 A/10, in position 38°42'N, 15°18'E, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) sighted the conning tower of a surfaced submarine. An attack was started but this failed due to problems with the gyro compass which were hampering P 43 since that morning.
This was probably the Italian Galatea proceeding from Pola to Cagliari. The Italians reported nothing though. (3)
11 Nov 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) once again had switched patrol positions with HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and commenced to patrol in position 'C'.
At 0810 A/11, a schooner, proceeding under her mainsail only, was sighted. This schooner was eastbound and passed 5 miles to the south of the submarine.
At 0820 A/11, two more schooners were seen which were following the first schooner sighted.
At 0840 A/11, three more schooners were seen which were following the others.
At 0900 A/11, the first schooner hauled her wind and steered north for about 3 miles, followed at intervals of about 3000 yards by the others.
At 0950 A/11, two more schooners were seen to the south. The first schooner furled her sail and headed towards. P 43 proceeded to the north at periscope depth the evade.
At 1000 A/11, the second schooner seen at 0950 A/11 now followed the first.

At 1020 A/11, two single funnel destroyers in line abreast approached from the east. As by now all schooners were apparently intent on forming a circle, and two Cant Z.501 aircraft were sweeping in the vicinity it was concluded that P 43 must have been detected. Lt. Halliday decided to go to 170 feet and withdrew to the north at slow speed. Asdic conditions were very good. The destroyers HE was lost in the wake of P 43.
[We have been unable to identify these destroyers.]

At 1130 A/11, P 43 returned to periscope depth as nothing had been heard for nearly an hour. Two schooners were visible to the southward with their sails furled. Two flying boats were circling. P 43 commenced to return to the patrol position.
At 1204 A/11, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1022 A/11 was received which contained the following orders; Move 5 miles 090°, three cruisers and four destroyers northbound at 25 knots.
Course was set to comply with these orders.

The light cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi and Luigi di Savoia Duca degli Abruzzi (8th Cruiser Division), escorted by the destroyers Alpino, Bersagliere, Granatiere and Camicia Nera, sailed from Augusta at 0520 A/11-0550 A/11 and arrived in Messina at 1032 A/11-13000 A/11. Initially, they had sailed from Navarino at 1630 A/9-1840 A/9 and arrived in Augusta at 0700 A/10-0830 A/10. The cruisers remained in Messina for the next weeks.?
At 1330 A/11, P 43 commenced to patrol in the position ordered but nothing was sighted but aircraft.
At 2008 A/11, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1729 A/11 was received. In this signal P 43 was ordered to patrol off Cape San Vito.
Course was set to comply with these orders. (3)
12 Nov 1942 At 2100 A/12, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived in position 315° - Cape San Vito lighthouse - 5 miles and commenced to patrol near that position. (3)

13 Nov 1942 At 0055 A/13, off Cape San Vito, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) picked up the HE of fast motor craft approaching from the south. This HE passed to the east and then made off to the south again. It was a very dark night and nothing could be seen. The HE was very loud.
At 0152 A/13, the HE faded out to the south.
Between 0632 A/13 and 0642 A/13, seven moderately loud explosions were heard. P 43 was at that moment in position 345° - Cape San Vito - 3.8 miles.
At 0650 A/13, six moderately loud explosions were heard.
At 0950 A/13, P 43 withdrew to position 330° - Cape San Vito - 10 miles as the area was heavily patrolled by aircraft.
At 1135 A/13, on coming to periscope depth HMS P 43 was directly between two enemy floatplanes. Lt. Halliday considered the submarine had been sighted and he ordered to go to a depth to 120 feet.
At 1140 A/13, a series of loud explosions apparently fairly close. Swishing water noises were heard 4 to 6 seconds after the band. P 43 altered course to retire to the north.
At 1245 A/13, HMS P 43 returned to periscope depth. Five aircraft, including two JU.88's, were sighted circling to the south.
At 1305 A/13, after sea conditions had improved, HMS P 43 commenced to regain her patrol position.
At 1335 A/13, on coming up to periscope depth, a large flying boat flew very close overhead.
At 1900 A/13, HMS P 43 surfaced.
At 1952 A/13, in position 38°23'N, 12°50'E, two shapes were sighted bearing 315°. P 43 altered course towards to attack. The targets were moving fast to starboard.
At 1959 A/13, the vessels were identified as two destroyers, camouflaged with light and dark patches. This pattern was very effective. Range was only about a mile. P 43 dived.
At 2040 A/13, the destroyers, which had passed at a range of only 1000 yards, now disappeared on bearing 080° proceeding at a speed of 25 knots. P 43 surfaced.

It is possible this were not two destroyers but the motorship Città di Napoli (Italian, 5418 GRT, built 1930) escorted by the torpedo boat Clio sailed from Bizerte and reached Palermo at 2306 A/13. They were travelling at 16 knots. Could Halliday have mistaken the Città di Napoli for a destroyer? Possible, see a photo of her camouflaged. Also, he would have substantially overestimated their speed (25 v/s 16 knots). (3)
14 Nov 1942 At 0825 A/14, when in position 38°15'N, 12°53'E, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) sighted a motor schooner coming west along the coast route. P 43 retired a little to the north to avoid.

At 0835 A/14, four single-funnelled destroyers in quarterline were seen bearing 350° at a range of 4 miles. They were proceeding on a course of 270° at 24 knots. It is thought they were coming from Palermo.

These were the Italian destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Maestrale, Grecale and Vincenzo Gioberti which sailed from Palermo at 0704 A/14-0750 A/14 carrying troops (10th Regiment Bersaglieri) and stores to Tunisia. They arrived in Bizerte at 1609 A/14-1630 A/14.
At 1820 A/14, eleven distant explosions were heard, they were fairly loud.
At 1900 A/14, HMS P 43 surfaced.
At 2115 A/14, HE was heard bearing 075°. Course was altered towards to attack.
At 2119 A/14, a small vessel was sighted ahead which was altering course frequently. P 43 dived to avoid.
At 2142 A/14, the HE faded out to the north and P 43 surfaced. (3)

14 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1501 A/14, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Parthian (Lt.Cdr. D. St. Clair-Ford, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); Establish patrol line by 0400 A/15, 3, 337° - Marettimo Light - 8, 360°, 7, 270°, 30. P 43 in southern billet and P 37 in northern. (33)
15 Nov 1942 At 1800 A/15, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived in the ordered patrol position as in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1501 A/14. (3)

16 Nov 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) fired four torpedoes against ' what is identified as ' a medium-sized tanker west of Marettimo Island, Italy in position 37°57'N, 11°56'E. This was the naval Italian auxiliary F 135 / Cerere (1198 GRT, built 1920) proceeding from Palermo to Bizerta. One torpedo scratched her keel but failed to explode.
Cerere had sailed from Trapani at 1840 A/16 for Bizerte but after the attack she turned back and arrived in Trapani at 0405 A/17.
Between 0914 A/16 and 1130 A/16, groups of nine, five and three explosions were heard. These were fairly loud.
1325 A/16 - Sighted a destroyer, thought to be of the Folgore-class, bearing 110° at a range of 7000 yards. Enemy course was 020°, speed about 10 knots. No HE was heard.
[We have been unable to identify this destroyer.]

1400 A/16 - One motor schooner was sighted bearing 163° at a range of 10000 yards. This vessel passed close north-west of Marettimo and proceeded on course 235°. P 43 closed the track in the hope that more vessels would follow.
1610 A/16 - Sighted mast and smoke bearing 125° coming towards. Started attack.
1618 A/16 - The vessel was seen to be an Italian auxiliary armed trawler of the 'Gallipoli-type'. The attack was broken off.
1636 A/16 - Sighted what is thought to be the conning tower of a U-boat bearing 115°. Started attack.
1703 A/16 - The 'conning tower' was seen to be a motor torpedo boat which was now being joined by another and they came in line abreast formation. P 43 retired to the north.
This were most probably the Italian MAS 530 and MAS 554 which sailed from Trapani at 1510 A/16 for an A/S sweep and returned at 2105 A/16.
2132 A/16 - A darkened ship was seen approaching Mugnone Point (the north-west corner of Marettimo Island). Enemy course was 260°. Commenced to close to attack.

2140 A/16 - The vessel passed close round Marettimo and steered 220° going very fast, about 20 knots. P 43 could not close sufficiently to warrant firing torpedoes as the range could not be closed further than 6000 yards. When the ship was silhouetted against the moon it was seen to resemble a cross Channel packed boat. It is thought to be a 'Citta di ..... type of vessel'.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
2216 A/16 - Broke off the attack and commenced to close Mugrone Point in the hope of more traffic.
2224 A/16 - Sighted a vessel bearing 065°. Started attack.
2231 A/16 - Dived to complete the attack from submerged due to the bright moonlight.
2248 A/16 - The target was seen to be either a tanker or a 'tanker built cargo ship'.
2250 A/16 - In position 37°57'N, 11°56'E fired four torpedoes from 800 yards. One torpedo hit is claimed.
2253 A/16 - Surfaced. The vessel was seen to be listing and heading towards Marettimo Island.
2254 A/16 - The vessel opened fire with her stern gun from 1000 yards. Dived again.
2300 A/16 - Surfaced to clear the vicinity.
2311 A/16 - A destroyer making clouds of smoke appeared close round the south of Marettimo Island. Dived and altered course 30° to starboard to avoid.
0110 A/17 - The destroyer was doing 300 revs, passed overhead and then searched to the west. HE faded out to the west at 0138 A/17.

The destroyer was most likely one of the four that was escorting a convoy made up of the Italian transports Monginevro (5324 GRT, built 1940), Sestriere (7992 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyers Mitragliere, Velite and Bombardiere sailed from Naples at 0555 A/16-0700 A/16. The destroyer Legionario sailed from Naples at 1055 A/16 joined them at 1700 A/16. The convoy arrived in Bizerte at 0620 A/17-0700 A/17. (3)

16 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1619 A/16, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN). This signal was also repeated to HMS Parthian (Lt.Cdr. D. St. Clair-Ford, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); Collect report from patrol line, withdraw 20 miles north-west after dark and inform me on enemy traffic and route, torpedoes remaining and remarks of interest.

In a signal timed 1915 A/16, P 37 informed the Capt. S.10 of the following. This signal was also repeated to HMS Parthian and HMS P 43; Unable to collect report due to defective Asdic [S.S./T. communication was thus not possible]. Two motor torpedo boats course 200° only shipping sighted from my position. Numerous aircraft. Five torpedoes remaining. Torpedo boat only ship sighted leaving (Palermo?) westbound.

In a signal timed 2355 A/16, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to these three submarines; Move 36 miles 073° to establish identical patrol line from position 360° - Cape St. Vito - 3 miles by 0400 A/17. (34)

17 Nov 1942 At 0521 A/17, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) cleared her signal timed 2343 A/16 which contained the following; All torpedoes expended. One hit medium sized tanker 2255 A/16 last seen listing attempted to beach in the vicinity of Libeccio Point. U-Boat attacked 1710 A/5, no hits. Night traffic only seen Marittimo Pass 1 miles west course 240°. Arrival position 'A' 0130 A/18, end of the searched channel at 1030 A/19. (35)
18 Nov 1942 At 0506 A/18, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) dived and commenced the eastbound passage of Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) at 120 feet.
At 2009 A/18, HMS P 43 surfaced. (3)
19 Nov 1942 At 1445 A/19, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 1215 A/19. (3)

28 Nov 1942 At 1300 A/28, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the northern approaches to Tunis / Marettimo area.
She, and HMS Traveller (Lt.Cdr. D. St.Clair-Ford, RN), were escorted out until 1445 A/28 by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR). (30)
28 Nov 1942 For the daily positions of HMS P 43 during her 8th war patrol see the map below.
29 Nov 1942 At 0420 A/29, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) dived and commenced the westbound passage of Q.B.B. 255 (mined area).
At 2200 A/29, HMS P 43 surfaced and set course to patrol to the north of Tunis Bay. (3)
30 Nov 1942 At 0415 A/30, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) sighted Zembla Island and proceeded to patrol in position 37°13.5'N, 10°34'E.
Nothing was seen all day except a huge number of aircraft, mostly transport planes. (3)

1 Dec 1942 At 0725 A/1, in position 37°14'N, 10°33'E, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) sighted a small single masted vessel, possibly a minesweeper, bearing 285° at a range of 7 miles. Enemy course was 250°. Nothing else materialised though. A total of 165 transport aircraft and 5 JU.88's were sighted during the daylight hours.
At 2108 A/1, a darkened vessel was sighted bearing 010° at a range of 4000 yards. Started attack.

At 2110 A/1, the vessel was identified as a destroyer proceeding on a course of 240° at 160 revs (15 knots). Broke off the attack and kept head on. The destroyer did not see the submarine at a range of 3000 yards.

The 'destroyer' seen was probably either the torpedo boat Partenope or Perseo which were sweeping mines off Bizerte. They had sailed from Trapani at 0135 A/1. At 1932 A/1, they were in position 37°37'N, 10°47'E, minesweeping toward Bizerte and at 2130 A/1, they reversed course. At 0830 A/2, they were informed that the destroyer Nicoloso da Recco had been sunk in position 349° - Marettimo – 60 miles and proceeded toward the area [this was an error, da Recco survived the action]. However, at 0905 A/2, they sighted ten survivors from the steamer Aventino (Italian, 3974 GRT, built 1907) [sunk by Force Q] who were picked up by Partenope in position 37°45'N, 10°57'E. At 1033 A/2, they picked up 100 survivors and 13 bodies from the destroyer Folgore in position 37°47'N, 11°04'E. They arrived in Trapani at 1940 A/2. (3)

2 Dec 1942 At 0140 A/2, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) picked up the HE of a turbine engined vessel doing 150 revs bearing 080°. Commenced attack. The HE moved south and then north again and eventually faded in that direction.

At 0204 A/2, HE was again picked up on bearing 360° but this was a different vessel as it was from a reciprocating engine. Started attack and increased speed. On slowing down the HE was not heard again. Nothing could be seen.
At 0230 A/2, the attack was broken off.
At 0730 A/2, in position 37°15'N, 10°36'E, a Spica-class torpedo boat was seen bearing 120° at a range of 4000 yards. Enemy course was 040° at 12 knots.
This was most probably either Partenope or Perseo. [See the event for 1 December 1942 for more information.]

Between 1020 A/2 and 1245 A/2, in position 37°14'N, 10°34'E, two drifting white lifeboats were seen. No names or registry port were seen on them. They had green bands and red crosses on the side. [These must have been from the Italian hospital ship Città di Trapani (2467 GRT, built 1929) which had been sunk, most likely mined, the previous day.]
At 2156 A/2, in position 37°15'N, 10°36'E, sighted a dark vessel bearing 060°. Started attack. The vessel was doing 240 revs (22 knots).
At 2158 A/2, the vessel was identified as a destroyer steering a course of 235°. P 43 dived to avoid, apparently unseen.
[We have been unable to identify this 'destroyer'.]
At 2220 A/2, all was clear and P 43 surfaced and resumed charging. (3)
3 Dec 1942 At 0037 A/3, in position 37°14'N, 10°33'E, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) sighted a darkened vessel approaching from the direction of Zembla Island.
At 0040 A/3, the vessel was seen to come directly towards so P 43 dived. HE was picked up doing 220 revs.
At 0143 A/3, nothing further was heard so P 43 surfaced and resumed charging.
At 0730 A/3, in position 37°13'N, 10°35'E, a motor schooner was sighted bearing 110° at a range of 8000 yards. Enemy course was 020° at 4 knots.

At 0920 A/3, in position 37°13'N, 10°35'E, the same vessel, thought to be an A/S schooner, was seen bearing 070° at a range of 6000 yards. Enemy course was 270° at 3 knots. HMS P 43 altered course to 180° to avoid.
At 0950 A/3, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0845 A/3 was received in with P 43 was ordered to move to a new patrol position, bearing 340° - 5 miles from her current patrol position.
At 1120 A/3, smoke was sighted bearing 010°. Also HE was picked up on this bearing which remained steady.
At 1121 A/3, sixteen loud explosions were heard.
At 1128 A/3, the bearing of the HE was drawing to the west.
At 1133 A/3, two single funnel destroyers or torpedo boats and three aircraft was seen bearing 360° at a range of approximately 10000 yards. They were searching.

At 1149 A/3, in position 37°18'N, 10°33'E, four merchant vessels and two destroyers / torpedo boats were seen bearing 346° at an estimated range of 15000 yards. Also several aircraft were patrolling over the convoy. Enemy course was 260° apparently heading for Bizerta.
At 1150 A/3, several aircraft came very close and two destroyers were heading towards. P 43 went to 60 feet.
At 1200 A/3, the HE of the destroyers indicated they were closing. The HE of the convoy was fading. P 43 went to 120 feet and proceeded eastwards.

The convoy sighted was the one made up of the Italian transports Arlesiana (5702 GRT, built 1915), Achille Lauro (5209 GRT, built 1921), Campania (5247 GRT, built 1918) and the German transport Menes (5609 GRT, built 1926). The German transport Lisboa (1799 GRT, built 1911) was also to have been part of this convoy but she apparently did not sail, perhaps due to defects (?). The convoy sailed from Trapani at 1700 A/2-1814 A/2, escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Sirio (escort leader), Groppo, Uragano and Orione. At 0735 A/3, they were reinforced by two German S-boats (out of the following, S 58, S 60, S 56 and S 54, which had sailed from Bizerte at 2115 A/2). At 0230 A/3, the Italian torpedo boat Animoso sailed from Trapani to reinforce the escort. At 0830 A/3, she sighted the convoy and took her place in the screen.

At 1037 A/3, in approximate position 37°26'N, 10°43'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) fired four torpedoes at two of the merchant vessels that were overlapping from very long range, 9000 yards. No hits were obtained. it had not been possible to close the range further.

At 1138 A/3, Achille Lauro and Arlesiana, escorted by Orione and Animoso, were detached to Tunis. At 1000 A/3, the torpedo boat Antonio Mosto sailed from Tunis to join the escort of the Tunis section. At 1225 A/3, she was hit by a torpedo from HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) which failed to explode. Shortly after, she joined the convoy.
So when HMS P 43 sighted the convoy at long range, it must have been in the process of splitting up.

Campania and Menes, escorted by Sirio and Uragano, proceeded to Bizerte. At about the same time, the torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori and two German S-boats were sighted and reinforced the Bizerte section. Giuseppe Sirtori had sailed from Bizerte at 0855 A/3 for minesweeping duty but was diverted to this new task. At 1235 A/3, Groppo rejoined them and was sent to escort the Tunis section, while Animoso was ordered to switch back to the Bizerte section. At 1330 A/3, Menes hit a mine and blew up. The four S-boats remained in the area to pick up the survivors. Campania escorted by Sirio, Uragano and Animoso arrived in Bizerte at 1610 A/3-1703 A/3.
Achille Lauro and Arlesiana, escorted by Groppo, Orione, Antonio Mosto and Giuseppe Sirtori, arrived in Tunis at 1715 A/3-1737 A/3.
At 1230 A/3, six fairly distant explosions were heard.
At 1240 A/3, HE of the destroyers faded on bearing 310°.

At 1300 A/3, P 43 returned to periscope depth. Numerous aircraft were patrolling in the area, rather close. No surface vessels were seen. Returned to 60 feet and proceeded in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0436 A/3 in which the submarines were ordered to retire from the area after the convoy had been attack or had passed.
At 1830 A/3, HMS P 43 surfaced and commenced charging.
At 1945 A/3, the passage though Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) passed Pantelleria was commenced.
At 2257 A/3, course was altered to the northwards as the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1551 A/3 indicated that submarines would be ordered in that direction.

At 2313 A/3, a small darkened vessel was seen bearing 270° at fairly close range. This may well be another submarine following the ordered route but it could also have been a small destroyer / torpedo boat. P 43 dived. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 2357 A/3, the HE of this vessel (220 revs) faded out on bearing 210°. P 43 surfaced and resumed charging. (3)

3 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2359 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); P 217 and P 219 proceed to the Naples area with despatch by proceeding on surface by day at the Commanding Officers discretion to patrol within 8 miles of the following positions; P 217, 40°47'N, 13°48'E and P 219, 40°35'N, 14°08'E. HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) is expected to join you shortly.

P 37, P 43 and P 48 proceed with despatch but submerged by day to form a patrol line (operation B.S.). 3 - 350° -Cape San Vito, with the first submarine 4 miles from Cape San Vito and the other submarine spaced 5 miles apart.
P 45 return to establish patrol approximately 40° from Plane Island lighthouse in position selected by you for interception of Bizerta traffic. (36)

3 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0438 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); On completion of attacks [on an expected convoy] you are to withdraw either the the north-east to the Tyrrhenian Sea or via the route of HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN) as in my 0005 A/1 to the south-east of Pantelleria.

[HMS Trooper's route had been through the following positions according the signal timed 0005 A/1 from the Capt. S.10; 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E), 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E), 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) and 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E).]

In a signal timed 0451 A/3, these submarines were sent the following orders; Assume the following dispositions forthwith; P 219, 37°28'N, 11°00'E. P 37, 37°24'N, 10°51'E. P 45, 37°13'N, 10°25'E. P 48 and P 43, remain in your present ordered positions. Position, course and speed of the enemy convoy at 0205 A/3, 37°50'N, 11°38'E, 235°, 8 knots. Convoy of four merchant ships and five destroyers.

In a signal timed 0845 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 37, HMS P 43 and HMS P 48; Move with utmost despatch from ordered position as follows; P 37, 10 miles 305°. P 48, 5 miles 305°. P 43, 5 miles 340°. (36)
4 Dec 1942 At 0040 A/4, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2347 A/3 ordering her to patrol off Cape San Vito. Course was set to comply with these orders.
At 0548 A/4, in position 37°58'N, 11°17.5'E, a white light was seen bearing 050° at a range of about 10 miles. Course was altered towards to investigate.
At 0621 A/4, HMS P 43 dived for the day as it was impossible to close. Nothing further was seen. The port generator was giving trouble.
At 0745 A/4, in position 38°05'N, 11°28'E, a large merchant vessel of about 12000 tons was seen steering a course of 235°. It looked like a hospital ship but appeared to be escorted. Started attack.

By 0820 A/4, HMS P 43 was 2500 yards off track. The vessel was seen to be escorted by a white painted Fiat RS 14 floatplane well ahead, one single-funnel destroyer / torpedo boat to starboard and one other unidentified vessel, probably heavily camouflaged, to starboard. Continued to close.

At 0833 A/4, while taking range, which was now 9000 yards, the target was seen to swing rapidly away, and head to a new course of about 030°. The aircraft at that time was on the further bow. The port destroyer / torpedo boat was coming towards fast and the starboard escort was also pointing towards.
At 0834 A/4, P 43 went to 90 feet and shut off for depth charge attack.
At 0840 A/4, HE indicated the enemy to be hunting astern at close range.
Between 0918 A/4 and 0920 A/4 two sounding charges were dropped. The enemy increased speed.
At 0928 A/4, four heavy explosions occurred right astern judging by the enemy's HE. P 43 was shaken but sustained no damage.
At 0930 A/4, both vessels appeared to stop astern to listen. The noise of their auxiliary machinery was plainly audible.
Between 1028 A/4 and 1030 A/4, two sounding charges were dropped but further away than the first pair.
1137 A/4, P 43 returned to periscope depth. Three JU.88's were searching to the south, otherwise all was clear. P 43 resumed the passage to her new patrol area.

The possible hospital ship seen may have been the German transport Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the Italian destroyers Granatiere and Saetta. Initially, Ankara, escorted by the Italian destroyers Saetta and Alpino, had sailed from Taranto at 0110 A/2-0200 A/2 and arrived at Messina at 0058 A/3-0124 A/3. Ankara, now escorted by the Italian destroyers Saetta and Granatiere, sailed from Messina at 1535 A/3-16185 A/3. At 1825 A/3-1829 A/3, the Italian torpedo boats Partenope and Perseo, both equipped with sonar, sailed from Trapani and joined the Ankara-convoy at 0245 A/4 and took position ahead. At 0359 A/4, Perseo got a sonar contact and left the convoy to hunt the submarine, nothing developed, and she returned to Trapani at 1104 A/4. At 0640 A/4, it was the turn of Partenope to leave the convoy, and she arrived at Trapani at 0820 A/4. At 0912 A/4, the convoy was attacked unsuccessfully by HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN). The alert was given by three escorting Italian fighters, which machine-gunned the sea. One torpedo missed Granatiere, 15 metres parallel to her starboard side, a second missed her stern by 10 metres, and missed the Ankara ahead. A third torpedo was observed to miss the Ankara on her starboard side. No counter-attack followed. At 1215 A/4, Supermarina ordered them to change their destination to Tunis. At 1316 A/4, one German S-boat (motor torpedo boat) joined the escort, followed at 1340 A/7 by three more (these were S 60, S 57, S 59 and S 54). The convoy arrived at Tunis at 1655 A/4-1714 A/4.
At 1620 A/4, masts were sighted bearing 007°. Enemy course was 170°. Started attack and altered course to 105° to intercept.
At 1645 A/4, the contact was seen to be two merchant vessels of about 7000-8000 tons escorted by two single-funnel destroyers or torpedo boats. Also a Fiat RS 14 floatplane was patrolling overhead.
At 1653 A/4, the vessels were approaching on an almost steady bearing. It was estimated P 43 could be about 600 yards on the port beam of the selected target at 1740 A/4.
At 1700 A/4, the air escort made a complete circle around the convoy and then flew away disappearing in the direction of Marettimo Island.

At 1714 A/4, without any apparent reason, both merchant vessels turned away to starboard when the range was about 8500 yards. The nearest destroyer / torpedo boat was making a cloud of smoke and was heading just astern of P 43. The other destroyer was not sighted in the fading light.
At 1715 A/4, the convoy was almost stern on. The destroyer was getting close. Broke off the attack and went to 90 feet and shut off for depth charging. Altered course to 360°.
At 1717 A/4, a loud explosion was heard. The submarine was shaken. HE of both destroyers / torpedo boats was heard to stop. HE of the merchant vessels was still audible but fading.
At 1718 A/4, the destroyers / torpedo boats were heard to stop and start. One of them than made off at high speed. The other one was not audible, probably that vessel was in the submarines wake.
At 1945 A/4, P 43 returned to periscope depth. Nothing more had been heard. On finding nothing in sight she surfaced.
The convoy sighted was a northbound one. Apparently they were proceeding southwards when sighted but most likely only briefly.

The convoy sighted was most likely the one made up of the Italian transports Sant'Antioco (5048 GRT, built 1919) and Honestas (4,959 GRT, built 1920) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Groppo and Orione. They had sailed from Tunis at 0215 A/15-0300 A/15 and were proceeding at 7 knots when, at 1120 A/4, they met the Ankara convoy on opposite course. At 1128 A/4, they heard two explosions ahead on the port side, believed to be aircraft bombs. At 1144 A/4, in position 37°42'N, 10°54'E, Groppo obtained a good sonar contact on the port side and ordered the convoy to turn to starboard while she hunted the submarine and dropped a pattern of depth charges. The echo appeared to be stationary, with a depth of about 200 metres, and was believed to be a wreck. The torpedo boat had few depth charges left, and she rejoined the convoy. At 1404 A/4, Orione signalled that her sonar had broken down, and Groppo ordered her to take station astern of the convoy. At 1717 A/4, in position 300° - Marettimo – 22 miles, a muffled explosion was heard (this explosion was also heard by the submarine) and Sant'Antioco reported to have seen a torpedo track, and it was believed to have exploded at the end of its run. At 0951 A/5, Orione reported having repaired her sonar and she resumed her station ahead of the convoy. At 1516 A/5, she signalled that she was short of water and was detached to Naples where she arrived at 1940 A/5. At 1522 A/5, in position 219° - Punta Carena (Capri) 6 miles, Sant'Antioco blew two whistles to signal a submarine. Groppo took up the hunt and, at 1531 A/5, obtained an echo. Four minutes later, a pattern of nine depth charges was delivered, and no more echo was heard. At 1800 A/5, Groppo was informed that Naples was under an air raid. At 1830 A/5, they obtained confirmation of this and reversed course. At 18359 A/5, the raid was over, and they resumed course. They arrived in Naples at 2142 A/5.
At 2000 A/4, passage to the new patrol area was resumed, charging on one generator. The port generator was still giving trouble and a thorough investigation was carried out to determine the cause.
At 2145 A/4, a clearly marked lighted hospital ship was seen. It was a big vessel.

At 2214 A/4, when about 5 miles off the hospital ship turned stern on, almost reversing her course. There was no chance the submarine could have been sighted as it was a very dark night and the moon had not risen. Most strange and unaccountable like the earlier events of this day.

There are two possibilities; 1) The Italian hospital ship Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) sailed from Tripoli at 1630 A/2, and arrived in Naples at 0750 A/5. However, she would have had to maintain a constant speed of nearly 20 knots, which is rather doubtful. 2) The Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) sailed from Naples at 1040 A/4, and arrived in Tripoli at 0830 A/6.
It seems thus more likely the hospital ship sighted was the Virgilio.
At 22407 A/4, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2213 A/4 was received which asked for a situation report.
At 2337 A/4, fast HE was reported on bearing 125°. 300 revolutions.
At 2342 A/4, a destroyer or torpedo boat passed close astern. P 43 dived. Two minutes later a second destroyer passed astern. Both slowed down and commenced to search to the south and west.
At 0020 A/5, HE faded. Remained dived for them moment thus unable to transmit the situation report.
[We have been unable to identify this 'destroyer' / 'torpedo boat'.] (3)

5 Dec 1942 At 0200 A/5, on board HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), the examination of the port generator was complete. If run it would be liable to irreparable damage and given the shortage of most spares at Malta it was decided to run it only in an emergency.
At 0250 A/5, P 43 surfaced and proceeded. Also the battery charge was resumed.
At 0438 A/5, the situation report to the Capt. S.10 was cleared including information on the generator breakdown. P 43 cleared the area in which the signal was transmitted at 7 knots.
At 0517 A/5, a destroyer or torpedo boat was sighted bearing 065° at close range. P 43 dived.

At 0520 A/5, the HE of two destroyers / torpedo boats doing 260 revs (24 knots) and one fast reciprocating engine (240 revs) were picked up. These vessels were apparently searching to the east, possibly in the D/F position of the W/T report. This was the fifth case of apparent detection without being sighted, and the only one with any explanation. Continued to close the new patrol position.

The vessels sighted were possibly from the convoy made up of the transports Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) and Sestriere (7992 GRT, built 1942), escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Sirio, Uragano and Animoso. This convoy had sailed from Bizerte at 1600 A/4-1700 A/4 at 14 knots and arrived in Naples at 1834 A/5-2315 A/5.
At 0945 A/5, P 43 was put deep for 15 minutes by a patrolling Cant. Z.506B flying boat which was searching in the vicinity.
At 1204 A/5, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1058 B/5 was received which orders all submarines on patrol to withdraw 50 miles from the coast. Course was set accordingly to 325°.
Between 1549 A/5 and 1705 A/5, a series of 39 heavy distant explosions were heard.
At 2054 A/5, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1745 A/5 was received which ordered P 43 to return to Malta via HMS Troopers route. Commenced the return passage steering to pass 35 miles off Marettimo.
[The route of HMS Trooper was as follows; Position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E), Position 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E), Position 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) and Position 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E).] (3)

5 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1058 A/5, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); Withdraw 50 miles from the coast, orders will follow. (36)
6 Dec 1942 While passing well to the west of Marettimo, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) sighted nothing except a few aircraft.

At 1852 A/6, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1530 A/6 was received which mentioned a possible compromise of the route of HMS Trooper through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area). An alternative route at least four miles different was worked out which was as follows; Position 'A' (37°31'N, 11°16'E), Position 'B' (37°17.5'N, 11°11'E) (cutting across the previous route), Position 'C' (37°08'N, 11°24'E), Position 'D' (37°02'N, 11°28'E) (clearing 150 fathoms on both sides), Position 'E' (36°42'N, 11°31'E). (3)
7 Dec 1942 At 0130 A/7, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) entered Q.B.B. 255 (mined area).
At 0323 A/7, Cape Bon was sighted.
At 0530 A/7, HMS P 43 went to 150 feet only coming up for W/T routines.
At 1104 A/7, a distant 'swishing' explosion was heard, thought to be a mine exploding.
At 1205 A/7, while at periscope depth for the 1200 hours W/T routine a JU. 87B dive bomber was seen heading for Pantelleria.
at 1445 A/7, the south-east limit of Q.B.B. 255 was cleared. The route figured out was not patrolled by the enemy and possibly clear of mines.
At 2004 A/7, HMS P 43 surfaced. (3)

7 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1202 A/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); Following sailed for Bone 1600 A/7. 1) HMS Dido (Capt. J. Terry, RN) close south of Sicily. She is warned of your positions. 2) 4 M.T.B's [this were HMMTB 260, HMS MTB 262, HMS MTB 266 and HMS MTB 316] north of Pantellaria via the approaches to Tunis and Bizerta. They are ordered not to attack submarines and are warned of Rorqual's high silhouette. (36)
9 Dec 1942 At 1315 A/9, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN), which had been out exercising, were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) which had joined around 1100 A/9.

During this patrol several convoys had been sighted and attacks were started. These however all had to be abandoned when P 43 appeared to have been sighted. P 43 also had been attacked with depth charges several times. When P 43 returned to Malta Lt. Halliday asked to be relieved of command. His confidence was badly shaken and he needed a rest.
P 43 returned to Malta with a defective port generator for which she was taken in hand at the dockyard. (3)
29 Dec 1942 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Malta together with HMS Jervis (Capt. A.L. Poland, CB, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN) and HMS Javelin (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Alliston, DSC, RN).
Another source, ADM 199/424, Malta war diary 1942, gives HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) instead of HMS Jervis. (37)
31 Dec 1942 Around 1300 A/31, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean).

She was ordered make rendezvous with HMS P 311 (Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO and 2 Bars, RN) at 0400 A/4, three nautical miles north of Ustica Island. Unbeknown to them HMS P 311 meanwhile had been mined and sunk off the Island of Tavolara. (3)
31 Dec 1942 For the daily positions of HMS P 43 during her 9th war patrol see the map below.

3 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1203 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN), HMS P 311 (Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), HMS Thunderbolt (Lt.Cdr. C.B. Crouch, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN); Take the following action; P 43 move to a waiting position 090° - Ustica - 6 miles (38°42'N, 13°24'E). P 46, if you have personnel on board withdraw to position 38°40'N, 12°40'E and signal report of their operation and yours in one time pad after dark. If you have no extra personnel proceed to position 38°30'N, 12°00'E and report after dark thence proceed via Cape Milazzo northward to operate to the south of Naples off the Bocca Piccolo. Thunderbolt set course to pass through position 38°50'N, 12°00'E to arrive in position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E) at 0800 A/4. Thence by normal route to rendezvous with minesweepers at the end of the searched channel at 0800 A/6. Trooper to follow Thunderbolt's route 24 hours later to arrive at Malta at 0800 A/7. P 311 if transfer of Lt. Kerr allowed follows 24 hours behind Trooper to arrive Malta 0800 A/8. (38)

4 Jan 1943 At 0615 A/4, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN), which had arrived off Ustica the previous day, called HMS P 311 (Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO and 2 Bars, RN) on S.S./T. but there came no reply. The rendezvous had been arrange for 0400 A/4.
P 43 waited in the rendezvous position until after dark/6 after which she proceeded to the pickup point of the Charioteers which should have been launched by P 311.
During the night of 5/6 January 1943, P 43 tried to pass a signal regarding the situation to the Capt. S.10 but this failed.

At 1830 A/6, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1351 A/6 was received. This signal contained orders which were to be carried out in case Lt. Kerr could not be transferred. P 43 then left the Ustica area for the recovery position off north-west Sardinia.
During the night of 6/7 January 1943 the attempt to pass a signal again failed initially and then the W/T set malfunctioned.

A signal was finally passed at 0416 A/8. This was P 43's signal timed 2001 A/7 which stated the following; Position 38°37'N, 10°06'E. Am proceeding in accordance with the orders in your 1351 A/6. P 311 did not appear. Unable to transmit previously. (39)

5 Jan 1943 In the evening HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) tried to pass a signal to Capt. S.10 reporting the non-arrival of P 311 at the rendez-vous. All attempts to pass this signal failed. It turned out that P 43's W/T equipment was defective.

At 0005/7, P 43 received a signal ordering her to make a situation report. This was successfully passed at 0416/8. In the evening of the 8th P 43 was ordered to proceed towards the Gulf of Asinara to recover the Chariot crews which participated in Operation Principle. (3)

5 Jan 1943 At 2100 A/5, in approximate position 39°09'N, 13°00'E, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a small craft, thought to be a motor torpedo boat at a range of 2 miles. P 43 dived to evade. No HE was heard.
At 2235 A/5, HMS P 43 surfaced. All was clear. (3)

10 Jan 1943 During the night of 10/11 January 1943, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sent out a Folbot manned by S.Lt. Palmer and Surgeon Crowley to take off the Chariot crews of Operation Principle (La Maddalena part). Two recovery positions were inspected but nobody was seen. The Folbots were recovered and P 43 set course towards Malta.
1407 A/10 - Arrived in position 2 miles - 360° - Stagnone Cove.
1823 A/10 - Surfaced.
2120 A/10 - Got bot two Folbots on deck through the fore hatch.
2130 A/10 - Trimmed down.
2230 A/10 - In the launching position, one mile north of Stagnone Cove.
2240 A/10 - Launched one Folbot. P 43 withdrew one mile to the the north-west.
0135 A/11 - The Folbot returned. S.Lt. Parker had visited both agreed rendezvous position but had sighted nobody.
0145 A/11 - Secured both Folbots below. Set course to commence the homeward passage. (3)

11 Jan 1943 At 2134 A/11, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 1846 A/11 in which she reported the result of the operation to the Capt. S.10. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (3)
16 Jan 1943 Around 1455 A/16, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (3)
19 Jan 1943 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) is docked in No.1 dock graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (40)
24 Jan 1943 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) is undocked. (40)
31 Jan 1943 At 1300 A/31, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Gabes.
1 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 1947 A/1, the Capt. S.10 sent the following order to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN); Do not go south of a line 070° from Zarzis.
This signal was received by P 43 at 2035 A/1. (41)

2 Feb 1943 At 2114 A/2, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1143 A/2, which contained the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) has returned to Malta. After dark tonight shift patrol to the Gulf of Hammamet between 36°00'N and 36°40'N. Keep east of 12°00'E between 33°00'N and 36°00'N. (41)

5 Feb 1943 At 0429 A/5, in position 36°23'N, 11°01'E, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a bright light to the northward which proved to be a southbound vessel lit as a hospital ship. P 43 turned to the eastward and the vessel passed about 4 miles inshore of her. When of El Maâmoura the hospital ship switched on a powerful searchlight towards the shore and then turned to the northward. The hospital ship was sweeping with her searchlight between herself and the shore. This 'strange' hospital ship went north and was lost to sight around 0600 A/5.
At 1556 A/5, in position 36°39'N, 11°00'E, a mast was sighted bearing 030°.
At 1607 A/5, P 43 started an attack.
At 1615 A/5, the target was seen to be a yacht correctly marked as a hospital ship and the attack was completed as a dummy one. This was the same vessel as had been seen at 0429 A/5.
At 2151 A/5, the 'hospital ship' was seen again. Judging by the times at which she passed P 43, the vessel is believed to be patrolling between Kelibia and El Maâmoura.

The hospital sighted on this day was most probably the Italian hospital ship Capri (630 GRT, built 1930) which sailed from Trapani at 1630 A/4 and arrived in Pantelleria at 1645 A/6. She was often used to rescue airmen who had ditched in the sea. (3)

6 Feb 1943 At 1408 A/6, east of El Maâmoura, in approximate position 36°26'N, 10°56'E, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) again sighted yesterday's 'hospital ship' bearing 358°. Another dummy attack on this vessel was made. (3)
7 Feb 1943 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) attacked a motor barge (landing vessel) in the Hammamet anchorage with one torpedo. The torpedo most likely got stuck in the bottom.
The target was probably the barge T 25, which sailed from Sousse at 1000 A/7 at 6 knots for Tunis and anchored in Hammamet at 1600 A/7.

1440 A/7 - In position 36°21'N, 10°38'E sighted the bridge of a motor barge bearing 240°. Enemy course was 020°. She was hugging the coast. P 43 followed the barge towards Hammamet anchorage. Several more vessels were sighted there. It was decided to fire a torpedo at a lighter.

1744 A/7 - Fired one torpedo from No.1 tube. The torpedo was set to 2 feet and was heard to run for 30 seconds but no track could be seen through the periscope. It is thought the torpedo got stuck in the bottom. (3)

8 Feb 1943 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sank the Italian barges Carlo P. and Angela with gunfire off Hammamet, Tunisia. A third, Luigi Verni, was damaged and beached but was a constructive total loss.

The Luigi Verni (58 GRT, built 1912), Carlo P. (64 GRT, built 1893) and Angela (56 GRT, built 1897) had sailed from La Goulette at 1530 A/5 for Sousse. Due to engine defects of Luigi Verni, speed was limited to 3 knots. As their order was to arrive at Sousse by daylight, they anchored in the Gulf of Hammamet during the night of 7/8 and had just resumed their march on the morning of 8 February when they were attacked. After Luigi Verni had come under fire by a few rounds, the submarine directed its fire on Angela which was hit by the 2nd round in the ammunition box of her machine-gun, detonating them. In the meantime, her captain had stopped the engine. The crew took to the lifeboat, and she quickly sank. Angela had a crew of nine and was carrying 85 tons of cement. Carlo P. was also sunk. There were no casualties.
0552 A/8 - Dived.
0910 A/8 - In position 36°12'N, 10°31'E sighted three motor barges were sighted inshore steering 190°. Speed of the enemy was 6 knots. Closed up for gun action.

0928 A/8 - Surfaced and opened fire on the last barge in line which crew had abandoned ship on surfacing of P 43. Within a minute after surfacing all three barges stopped. Ten rounds were fired at the last barge in line. Target was then shifted to the second barge thereby considerably hastening its abandonment. Eleven rounds were fired at this barge before fire was shifted to the first barge. After three hits she caught fire and sank rapidly. Since the other barges were still afloat P 43 turned an opened fire on the other barges again. The last barge of the line soon rolled over and sank.
0945 A/8 - Dived and retired to the eastward.
1845 A/8 - Surfaced. (3)
9 Feb 1943 At 1320 A/9, in position 36°30'N, 10°54'E, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a sail bearing 020°. P 43 closed to investigate.
At 1340 A/9, four very small sailing vessels were seen beating down the coast. They were not molested. (3)

10 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 1322 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN); During daylight tomorrow 11th, P 44 patrol in northwest corner of your area. P 43 patrol in [position garbled in the signal] after 0100 A/11th. (42)

11 Feb 1943 HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sank the small German transport (coaster) Jaedjoer (former Dutch, 309 GRT, built 1937) with gunfire off Ras Mahmur, Tunisia in position 36°26'N, 10°55'E.

Jaedjoer, which was carrying a cargo of ammunition, had sailed from Palermo at 0230 A/10 for Sousse escorted by the torpedo-boat Pallade. At 1206 A/10, off Trapani, they were joined by the Skotfoss (former Norwegian, 1465 GRT, built 1917).

At 0600 A/11, in poor visibility, Jaedjoer lost contact with the other two vessels. At 1630 A/11, Skotfoss and Pallade anchored off Hammamet to wait for the weather to improve. They raised anchor at 0615 A/12 and reached Sousse at 1100 A/12.
1744 A/11 - In position 36°26'N, 10°55'E sighted a small but heavily laden merchant ship bearing 178°. Range was about 2 nautical miles, enemy course 165°.
1807 A/11 - Surfaced to give chase.
1824 A/11 - Opened fire with the gun from 1000 yards. The first two rounds scored hits. Two lifeboats had left the ship by now. Eleven more hits were then obtained.
1840 A/11 - A small fire had started on board the coaster but it did not appear to be sinking.

1846 A/11 - Fired one torpedo to finish her off but due to yawing in the seaway it missed ahead of the target. The fire then increased in intensity. As it seemed certain the target would be destroyed by fire P 43 cleared the area. A few minutes later several explosions occurred on the target.
1910 A/11 - The target blew up with a terrific explosion.

2154 A/11, Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1137 A/11, which contained the following orders for P 43; Leave patrol after dark/12 and pass 20 miles north of Linosa through position 36°05'N, 14°05'E to position '6' at 0830 A/14. (3)

12 Feb 1943 At 1903 A/12, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) surfaced after a day of submerged patrol. She then left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1137 A/11. (3)
14 Feb 1943 Around 1045 A/14, HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) which had joined ariund 0750 A/14. (3)

24 Feb 1943 At 1300 A/24, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Hammamet, west of 12°00'E and between 26°25'N / 26°40'E.
En-route to her patrol area she was to pass to the north of Linosa.
For the daily positions of HMS Unison during this patrol see the map below.

25 Feb 1943 At 0440 A/25, in position 36°25'N, 11°49'E, HMS P Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 260° at a range of about 4 miles. Unison altered course to 210° and closed at full speed. Within two minutes of the first sighting the outline of this submarine looked familiar and by 0450 A/25 there was no doubt that this submarine was actually HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN) on passage to Haifa.
At 0452 A/25, HMS Unison dived for the day.
HMS Rorqual did not report anything in that position and at that time but there is little doubt it was was indeed this submarine as she was passing through that area around this time. (3)

26 Feb 1943 At 1245 A/26, off of El Maâmoura, in approximate position 36°28'N, 10°53'E, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a very small sailing vessel to the southward heading towards El Maâmoura. Did not molest this very small vessel.
At 1530 A/26, two beached schooners were sighted near Nabeul. (3)
27 Feb 1943 Between 0730 A/27 and 1030 A.27, while patrolling near El Maâmoura, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) heard 28 fairly loud explosions.

At 2012 A/27, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2310 A/26 was received which contained the following orders; A) Extend your patrol to an area formed by joining following points; 1) 37°05'N, 11°25'E, 2) 37°05'N, 11°59'E, 3) 36°12'N, 11°25'E, 4) 36°12'N, 10°16'E.

B) HMS Saracen (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN) on passage to Malta passes through the north-east corner of your area during the night of 28 Feb. / 1 Mar. Do not attack submarines from dark/28 until dawn/2.

At 2022 A/27, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1123 A/27 was received which contained the following information / orders; Surface forces operating tonight in Ras Mahmur area approaching south of Q.B.B. 286 and Q.K.B. 22 (mined areas) and returning north of it. They will not attack submarines west of 12°00'E. Keep in your south-west corner if possible, position 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E) is not to be approached within 10 miles.

[The surface forces referred to in the signal were two destroyers of Force K which were to leave Malta at 1530 A/27 to carry out a sweep between the Tunisian Coast and Pantellaria during the night. The destroyers in question were HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) and HMS Javelin (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Alliston, DSC, RN). They returned to Malta shortly after 0800 A/28 having sighted nothing.] (41)
28 Feb 1943 At 0506 A/28, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) dived for the day.

She then closed Sousse to conduct a periscope reconnaissance of the harbour. An unladen merchant vessel of about 1500 tons [this was most likely the transport Skotfoss (German, former Norwegian, 1465 GRT, built 1917)] was laying in the harbour and the foremast and funnel of what appeared to be a trawler were visible further inside the harbour. The wreck of a medium sized merchant vessel, with masts and funnel showing, lies to the north-west of the town [this was most likely the wreck of the Macedonia (German, 2875 GRT, built 1922)].
At 1913 A/28, HMS Unison surfaced. (3)
1 Mar 1943 At 0452 A/1, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) dived for the day.
She then closed the Hammamet anchorage but found no shipping there. She then proceeded eastwards along the coast. (3)
4 Mar 1943 At 1200 A/4, in position 36°28'N, 10°54'E, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted four small sailing vessels heading towards El Maâmoura. They were not molested. (3)

5 Mar 1943 At 2025 A/5, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1021 A/5 which contained the following orders for her; Leave patrol as necessary to pass through position 36°03'N, 12°52'E and mae rendezvous with the minesweeper at the end of the searched channel at 0830 A/8. Be east of 12°00'E by dusk/7. (43)
6 Mar 1943 Between 1220 A/6 and 1240 A/6, when in approximate position 36°31'N, 10°55'E, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) heard 30 distant explosions.
At 1923 A/6, Unison surfaced after a day of submerged patrol. She then left patrol and set course for Malta. (3)
8 Mar 1943 At 1030 A/8, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 0830 A/8. (3)

18 Mar 1943 Around 1500 A/18, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the south and east Calabrian coast.

She was routed south of Q.K.B. 6 (mined area) through position 35°20'N, 15°28'E thence course 10° to her patrol area between lat. 37°20'N, and 39°20'N and north-west of a line 040° from position 37°20'N, 16°24'E.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unison during this patrol see the map below.
22 Mar 1943 At 1120 A/22, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a mast off Cape Spartivento. Course was altered to close to investigate.
At 1200 A/22, in position 37°58'N, 16°11'E, the mast was now seen to be the one of an armed trawler proceeding northward, close inshore.
The trawler was towing two rafts which were thought to be carrying couls for exploding magnetic mines. (3)

23 Mar 1943 HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian tanker Zeila (1835 GRT, built 1898) off Cape Spartivento in position 37°57'N, 16°10'E. Zeila was in convoy together with the Italian salvage vessel Artiglio (386 GRT, built 1908). They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Angelo Bassini and Antonio Mosto, the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2201 and UJ 2204 and several VAS-boats. Fourteen survivors, including six wounded, were picked up, ten were missing.

The convoy had sailed from Crotone at 0224 A/23. After Zeila blew up, Antonio Mosto dropped eight depth charges (two failed to explode). UJ 2204 and UJ 2201 continued the hunt. Artiglio, escorted by and Antonio Mosto proceeded onwards while a VAS boat picked up the survivors and transferred them to Angelo Bassini (escort leader) which arrived in Messina at 1730 A/23. Artiglio and Antonio Mosto followed at 1855 A/23.
0430 A/23 - Dived and patrolled down the coast to the north of Cape Spartivento.

0930 A/23 - Sighted smoke bearing 355°, which came from a trawler going down the coast close inshore. This trawler passed Unison at 1010 A/23 and appeared to be similar to the vessel seen the previous day but she was now not towing any rafts.

1210 A/23 - In position 37°57'N, 16°11'E, two motor A/S boats were seen bearing 160° at a range of 3 miles. They were going very slow to the eastwards on various courses. No A/S transmissions were heard and these boats were lost from sight at 1240 A/23.
1245 A/23 - Sighted smoke bearing 060°. Started attack.

1322 A/23 - The enemy was seen to be a convoy made up of two merchant vessels escorted by an older type torpedo boat, two large trawlers and two MAS-boats. A large number of aircraft were seen to be patrolling in the area.

1409 A/23 - Fired four torpedoes from a range of approximately 2000 yards. A hit was heard 1m 43s after firing the first torpedo. Unison meanwhile had gone to 90 feet but due to an error in drill the depth gauge was shut off. When opened up the depth was 280 feet and going down.
1412 A/23 - Unison hit bottom at a depth of 345 feet. She bounced off and then rose to 100 feet at which depth a good trim was caught.
1417 A/23 - Depth charging started.
1600 A/23 - By now eighty-eight depth charges had been dropped but none had been very close.
1745 A/23 - Depth charging ceased. A total of 133 had been counted. Only one light bulb had been broken as a result of the depth charging.
2213 A/23 - Surfaced.
2334 A/23 - Set course for the vicinity of Punta Stilo. (3)

24 Mar 1943 At 2045 A/24, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1717 A/24 which contained the following orders; Withdraw 60 miles from the enemy coast and at your discretion report the number of torpedoes fired. Thereafter return to your area.

Course was thus set to withdraw from the coast and at 0107 A/25, HMS Unison passed her reply to the Capt. S.10's signal in which she stated that she had obtained a torpedo hit on a 2000 tons motor vessel, probably a tanker, at 1409 A/23 in position 37°57'N. 16°10'E. The vessel is believed to have sunk. She also reported that she had four torpedoes remaining.
After transmitting the signal HMS Unison closed the coast to the west of Cape Rizzuto and patrolled in that vicinity. (3)

26 Mar 1943 At 0908 A/26, in position 39°08'N, 17°12'E, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted two motor A/S boats bearing 270° proceeding down the coast and apparently eventually entering Crotone.

During the forenoon, two schooners and an armed yacht were sighted. The yacht and the larger of the two schooners maintained an inshore patrol to the north and south of Crotone and could not be closely observed. The other schooner also conducted a patrol. By 1615 A/26 all three vessels were back in the harbour.

At 2100 A/26, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1155 A/26 was received which contained the following orders; Keep south of 38°15'N, after 0001 A/28 HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) will be operating in the area to the north of this latitude. My 1148 A/26 refers [This signal contained the route and patrol area of HMS Unbroken]. Cancel paragraph 6 in your sailing orders. My 1204 A/15 is to be observed [We have been unable to find the text of this signal]. (45)
28 Mar 1943 At 0211 A/28, between Cape Spartivento and Cape dell'Armi, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted the lights of two Swedish relief ships bearing 090°.
At 0248 A/28, the lights of two more Swedish relief ships were sighted. HMS Unison was aware of these ships being in the area and they were on their expected line of advance.
This were most likely the Swedish transports (relief ships) Formosa (7033 GRT, built 1921), Mongabarra (5279 GRT, built 1941) and Camelia (1688 GRT, built 1941).
At 0434 A/28, HMS Unison dived and patrolled off the coast to the east of Cape dell'Armi. (46)
29 Mar 1943 At 0620 A/29, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) left her patrol area and set course to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1058 A/27.

At 2223 A/29, east of Syracuse, in position 37°07'N, 15°51'E, HMS Unison sighted a submarine surfacing about 2000 yards on the starboard beam. Enemy course was about 045°. Started attack but soon the enemy turned away at speed and dived on a course of 020°.
At 2226 A/29, HMS Unison dived but no HE was picked up. By 2315 A/29 still nothing had been heard of the enemy and HMS Unison surfaced and continued her passage to Malta.
It was thought the submarine looked similar to the Italian Squalo-class, with a raised bow and a new type of smaller conning tower.

The submarine in question was in fact the Italian minelaying submarine Filippo Corridoni on a supply trip from Augusta to Lampedusa. The Italian also sighted a submarine at a range of 500 meters. The Italians turned away and dived to 40 meters. (3)
31 Mar 1943 At 1050 A/31, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) which had joined around 0820 A/31. (3)
10 Apr 1943 Around 1000 A/10, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the North of Sicily.

Before proceeding on patrol A/S exercises were carried out with HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) and HMS Paladin (Lt.Cdr. L.St.G. Rich, RN). Also a practice attack was carried out on HMS Nubian.

On completion of these exercises, HMS Pakenham (Cdr. B. Jones, DSC, RN), HMS Petard (Lt.Cdr. R.C. Egan, RN) also joined. These two destroyers were supposed to also have carried out A/S exercises with HMS Unison but all four destroyers made off to the westward. HMS Unison was ordered to wait until 1700 A/10 but if the Capt. D.12 in HMS Pakenham would not return, HMS Unison was to proceed in accordance with her previous orders. (3)
10 Apr 1943 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unison during her 13th war patrol see the map below.

11 Apr 1943 At 2056 A/11, to the north of Pantelleria, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) surfaced. The lights of a hospital ship were sighted bearing 270° at a range of 4 miles. The hospital ship was on a course of 0??°. [unreadable in the patrol report and unfortunately there is no log available to double check, course was most likely to the north-east.]
At 2100 A/11, a two-masted schooner was sighted bearing 220° at a range of 3000 yards. Enemy course was 300°. HMS Unison turned bows on.
At 2105 A/11, HMS Unison dived to avoid detection.
At 2156 A/11, HMS Unison surfaced. The schooner was no longer in sight. The lights of the hospital ship were lost to sight at 2230 A/11 on bearing 020°.
The hospital ship was most probably the Italian Laurana (391 GRT, built 1939) which sailed from Trapani at 1840 B/10 and returned at 1800 B/12.
At 2210 A/11, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1144 A/11 was received which ordered HMS Unison to patrol in zone 'A'. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (3)

12 Apr 1943 At 0838 A/12, west of Marettimo, in position 37°55'N, 11°30'E, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted six Siebel ferries and three barges bearing 180° at a range of about 5000 yards. Enemy course was 075°. These craft, with their shallow draught and powerful gun armament were not considered suitable targets.

The vessels sighted may have been the German Siebel ferries SF 86, SF 94, SF 183, SF 195, SF 213, SF 214 and the barges [???] J-B 032, J-B 041, J-B 060 and WB 027 which arrived at Marsala around 1700 B/12 from La Goulette.
At 0902 A/12, several bursts of machine gun fire were heard striking the water. (47)

13 Apr 1943 At 0018 A/13, near Marettimo Island, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted the wash of two motor torpedo boats crossing the stern on a course of 350°. The boats themselves were not seen but their wash was clearly visible about 6 miles away in the moonlight. Four minutes after sighting the wash was lost to sight.
At 0539 A/13, HMS Unison dived. Lt. Daniell decided to patrol to the west and north-west of Marettimo Island.
At 0915 A/13, faint HE was heard bearing 160°.
At 0938 A/13, the HE faded. Nothing had been sighted.
At 1037 A/13, the Officer of the Watch reported a large periscope bearing 020° at a range of one mile. No HE was picked up and the periscope was not seen again.
If this was indeed a periscope it was most likely one from HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN) on a storing trip from Algiers to Malta.
At 1200 A/13, HMS Unison was in position 38°02'N, 11°46'E.
At 1300 A/13, distant explosions were heard.
At 2053 A/13, HMS Unison surfaced.

At 2328 A/13, north of Marettimo, in position 38°14'N, 12°07'E, a lighted ship was sighted bearing 320°. This ship was subsequently seen to be a hospital ship proceeding on a course of 140°. Unison altered course to the westward to gain distance off track so as to be sure of being down moon when the ship would pass.
At 0035 A/14, the hospital ship was now almost stopped about 3000 yards away bearing 270°. Her silhouette could not be made out but she appeared to have a raised bow.
At 0039 A/14, a steady white light was sighted bearing 120°.
At 0043 A/14, HMS Unison dived. This white light seemed to be approaching and is thought to have been a pilot vessel coming from Trapani.
By 0200 A/14, the hospital ship was no longer visible through the periscope.
At 0206 A/14, HMS Unison surfaced. The hospital ship was seen bearing 180° but she disappeared soon afterwards.
The hospital ship was most likely the Italian Toscana (9429 GRT, built 1923) which sailed from Leghorn at 0445 B/12 and arrived in Bizerte at 2115 B/14. (3)

13 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 2228 A/13 the Capt. S.8 informed HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) of the following; 1) Weather permitting two Tribal-class destroyers leaving Bone at 1500 A/14 proceeding north of Galita Islands, thence through position 38°10'N, 10°56'E at 2200 A/14, thence sweeping on line 250° from movement turning back at 2359 A/14 if no target found and returning south of Galita Islands. 2) Two Hunt-class destroyers will carry out a sweep between Galita Islands and 38°10'N until daylight/15. 3) HMS Unison, keep clear to the north-east of these movements. 4) These surface forces are informed on HMS Unbending's movements.
[The two Tribal-class destroyers referred to were HMS Tartar (Cdr. St J.R.J. Tyrwhitt, DSC, RN) and HMS Ashanti (Lt.Cdr. J.R. Barnes, RN).]
The two Hunt-class destroyer referred to were HMS Bicester (Lt.Cdr. S.W.F. Bennetts, RN) and HMS Zetland (Lt.Cdr. J.V. Wilkinson, RN).] (48)

14 Apr 1943 At 0615 A/14, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) heard and saw two motor torpedo boats bearing 325° at a range of about 2 miles. Enemy course was 065° at slow speed. One altered course towards. HMS Unison dived to 60 feet.
At 0655 A/14, the HE faded on bearing 085°.
