At 0705 A/14, HMS Unison came to periscope depth. There was nothing in sight.
At 1200 A/14, HMS Unison was to the north-west of Marettimo Island, in position 38°06'N, 11°46'E.
At 2047 A/14, HMS Unison surfaced and set course to patrol off Cape San Vito. (3)
15 Apr 1943 Between 1140 A/15 and 1402 A/15, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) heard a number of distant explosions.
At 1200 A/15, HMS Unison's was to the west of Cape San Vito, in position 38°09'N, 12°37'E.
At 1328 A/15, the masts of a small vessel were sighted on bearing 130°. The vessel's hull was not seen but she was close inshore. The southbound vessel was last seen at 1420 A/15.

At 1529 A/15, three tank landing craft were seen proceeding on course 010° at a speed of 9 knots. A dummy attack was carried out on these craft which were all running light. Two more of the vessels were sighted at 1615 A/15 and all followed the same route round Cape San Vito. It was hoped that a good target might be following these craft but nothing developed.

This were most likely the Italian landing craft (F-boats) MZ 756, MZ 772, MZ 780, MZ 784 and MZ 794 which sailed from Trapani at 1200 B/15 and reached Termini Imerese [this is to the east of Palermo] at 2220 B/15.
At 2042 A/15, HMS Unison surfaced and set course for Zone 'C' as had been ordered by the Capt. S.8 in his signal timed 1748 A/14. (3)
16 Apr 1943 At 0529 A/16, to the east of Palermo Bay, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted two motor torpedo boats against the first light of dawn. Two minutes later HMS Unison dived.

At 0550 A/16, the HE of the motor torpedo boats faded after they had passed to the southward on a course of 250°. On coming to periscope depth nothing was seen. Course was then set to patrol to the north-west of Cape Cafalu but nothing more was seen all day. (3)
17 Apr 1943 At 0515 A/17, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) dived and closed Cape d'Orlando from the north-west.
At 0957 A/17, a funnel was seen bearing 160°. HMS Unison closed to investigate.
At 1002 A/17, two ships were sighted proceeding on course 270°. These were thought to be tankers. Course was altered to the south-west to close at full speed.

At 1016 A/17, the ships were now seen to be a large steam trawler and what is thought to be a motor trawler. Both ships had an AA gun bandstand forward on a stalk in the well-deck. The description of two 'Q'-ships seen recently by other submarines seemed to fit the latter vessel.
Enemy course was 250° but shortly afterwards they began manoeuvring and starting and stopping as if they suspected something.
At 1110 A/17, the enemy vessels made off on course 250° and soon disappeared from sight.

The vessels seen were the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2203 and UJ 2205 en-route from Messina (departed around 0300 B/17) to Palermo where they were to join a convoy around 0800 B/18. En-route they were to conduct an A/S sweep.

The convoy they were to was made up of the Italian tanker Bivona (former French Socombel, 1646 GRT, built 1930), which sailed from Palermo for Bizerte at 0850 B/18, escorted by UJ 2203, UJ 2205, VAS 207 and VAS 230. At 1106 B/18, UJ 2205 (600 tons, for-French Jacques Coeur, built 1933) was sunk by HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN). The convoy returned to Palermo. It sailed again at 0845 B/19, reinforced by UJ 2208, and this time Bivona was sunk by HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) at 1550 B/19.
At 1200 A/17, HMS Unison was in position 38°13'N, 14°41'E. [The patrol report gives position 38°13'N, 12°41'E but this is clearly a mistake.]

At 2058 A/17, HMS Unison surfaced and then proceeded to the northward into zone 'E' in accordance with the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1154 A/17. This signal had contained the following orders for P 43; Leave patrol through the north-west corner of zone 'C', thence through zones 'E' and 'D'. Pass north-west corner of zone 'A' at 2330 A/19 and through position 'P' at 0530 A/20 thence Malta as will be ordered by the Capt. S.10. (49)
18 Apr 1943 At 0317 A/18, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted the lights of a ship bearing 105°. This was seen to be a hospital ship proceeding on a course of 280°.

At 0329 A/18, HMS Unison dived. No HE could be heard and the hospital ship remained stopped until 0505 A/18 when it went ahead at 200 revs on a course of 280°. Meanwhile HMS Unison had closed submerged to investigate but the outline of the ship could not be seen through to periscope due to the glare of the hospital ship's lights. After dawn the hospital ship was almost hull down. It was thought the vessel may have been the Italian Principessa Giovanna (8389 GRT, built 1923) which was indeed correct. She had sailed from Messina at 1830 B/17 and arrived in Tunis ca. 19 or 20 April and sailed again from Tunis at 1435 B/20 for Leghorn.

At 0631 A/18, to the south of Alicudi Island, another hospital ship was sighted bearing 250°. A dummy attack was carried out on this ship which was steering a course of ?05° [course unreadable in the patrol report] at 12 knots. The vessel looked like the Francisco Crispi (7464 GRT, built 1926) but with the after funnel removed. The vessels name, which was pained in large letters on her bow, ended with the letters 'IA'. This ship disappeared from sight at 0725 A/18, still steering the same course.
This was the Italian Aquileia (9448 GRT, built 1914), which sailed from Tunis at 0630 B/17 and arrived in Bari at 0825 B/20.
At 0833 A/18, a distant explosion was heard.
Between 1106 A/18 and 1216 A/18, 43 distant explosions were heard.
Between 1302 A/18 and 1349 A/18, 19 distant explosions were heard.
Between 1436 A/18 and 1449 A/18, 10 more distant explosions were heard.
The explosions heard were from an enemy A/S hunt in which HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN), in the neighbouring area, was showered with depth charges.
At 2055 A/18, HMS Unison surfaced and proceeded to the westward.

At 2144 A/18, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1204 A/18 was received in which a new time was given at which HMS Unison was to pass position 'P'. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal and the patrol report does not mention the adjusted time.] (3)

19 Apr 1943 At 0005 A/19, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) received an aircraft reported giving the position, course and speed of two unidentified ships as 38°05'N, 11°55'E (north-west of Marettimo), 020°, 10 knots at 2340 B/18. HMS Unison increased speed to 10 knots and altered course to 290° as there was a chance of intercepting these ships after dawn.

At 0025 A/19, a second signal was received from the same aircraft timed 0010 B/19. This gave practically the same position as the previous signal but a course of 060°. The enemy was described as a merchant ship and a destroyer. Lt. Daniell considered this course more likely and it was decided to ignore the first signal and act on this signal.

These signal must have referred to the convoy made up of the Italian transport Mostaganem (former French, 1942 GRT, built 1921) and the Italian torpedo boat Tifone which was sunk at 0120 A/19 in position 38°11'N, 11°44'E by HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN).

The Mostaganem, escorted by the torpedo boat Tifone, had sailed from Bizerte at 0400 B/18 for Palermo. At 0130 B/19, Mostaganem was believed to have been torpedoed by enemy aircraft (actually by HMS Unrivalled) and sank in position 290° - Levanso - 20 miles. Most of the crew were picked up and Tifone arrived at Trapani at 0925 B/19.
At 0104 A/19, Ustica Island was seen bearing 260°.
At 0456 A/19, HMS Unison dived.
At 0521 A/19, distant explosions were heard.
At 0939 A/19, distant explosions were heard.
At 1101 A/19, distant explosions were heard.
At 1200 A/19, HMS Unison was in position 38°52'N, 12°30'E.

At 1550 A/19, two distant explosions were heard. Three minutes later a column of grey smoke was seen bearing 180°. From 1621 A/19 to 1850 A/19, 52 explosions were heard. Looked like HMS Unrivalled had made a successful attack.
This was indeed the case as this submarine had just sunk the Italian tanker Bivona (former French Socombel, 1646 GRT, built 1930).
At 2058 A/19, HMS Unison surfaced.
At 2217 A/19, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1039 A/19 was received 0530 A/21.
In an earlier signal timed 1009 A/18 the Capt. S.8 had ordered HMS Unison to wait in the southern half of Area 'D' until 2330 A/20. (3)

21 Apr 1943 HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Marco Foscarini (8073 GRT, built 1943) about 40 nautical miles west of Favignana, Italy in position 37°48'N, 11°32'E.

She was escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Ardimentoso and Libra and was en-route from Bizerta (departed 0330 B/21-0440 B/21) to Naples. Of the 120 crew members and passengers, 96 were picked up, including a German officer who later died of his wounds. Libra counter-attacked with depth charges and claimed the submarine as probably damaged. The two torpedo boats arrived at Trapani at 1805 B/21-1830 B/21.
0434 A/21 - north-west / west of Marettimo, sighted the lights of a ship bearing 270°. Not surprisingly it was yet another hospital ship proceeding on a course of 060°.
0510 A/21 - Dived 7 miles 060° from position 'P'.
1154 A/21 - In position 37°48'N, 11°32'E heard slow diesel HE bearing 230°. Went to periscope depth.

1157 A/21 - On coming to periscope depth sighted a large merchant vessel escorted by a torpedo-boat. Started attack in which four torpedoes were fired from 1100 yards. Two torpedo hits were heard after 1 min. and 1 min. 6 sec. after firing the first torpedo.
1213 A/21 - The escort started a counter attack but was never in contact. She dropped fourteen depth charges but none were close.
1312 A/21 - HE faded and A/S transmissions ceased. HMS Union continued her passage to Malta. (3)
23 Apr 1943 At 1140 A/23, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), were escorted in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) which had joined around 0845 A/23. (3)

4 May 1943 At 1400 B/4, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Sicily. Before she proceeded on patrol exercises were carried out with aircraft based at Malta.
During the exercises, which laded until around 1815 B/4, HMS Unison was escorted by HMML 134 (T/Lt. P.L. Butcher, RNVR).
HMS Unison was routed through positions 'R', 'Q' and 'P' keeping to starboard of the route, passing position 'P' at about 1600 B/6. (3)
4 May 1943 For the daily positions of HMS Unison during her 14th war patrol see the map below.

5 May 1943 In a signal timed 1154 A/5, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN); Pass position 'P' at 1500 A/6 and proceed to zone 'B' and zone 'E', west of 14°00'E.
In the same signal HMS Unison was also informed that three destroyers from Bone will be sweeping towards movement tonight returning to Bone on completion.

This referred to an anti-shipping sweep by the destroyers HMS Eskimo (Capt. J.W.M. Eaton, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Lookout (Lt.Cdr. A.G. Forman, DSC, RN) and ORP Blyscawica (Kmdr.ppor. (Cdr.) L. Lichodziejewski) in the Marettimo area. (50)

6 May 1943 At 0653 B/6, south-west of Marettimo Island, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a tank landing craft bearing 335° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 050°. This landing craft was lost from sight at 0715 B/6.
This was most likely the Italian landing vessel MZ 792 on passage from La Goulette to Trapani where it arrived at 1400 B/6.

Unfortunately, there is gap in the Supermarina diaries concerning her movements and we cannot add anything except she had initially arrived in Tunis at 1015 B/4 (with MZ 787, MZ 788, MZ 789 and MZ 793). At 1530 B/5, she was slightly damaged in an air raid. She must have sailed from Tunis during the night of 5/6 May. She sailed from Trapani at 0900 B/9 with MZ 787, MZ 798 and MZ 799 for Termini Imerese.
Between 0820 B/6 and 0833 B/6, a series of sharp explosions which sounded like small bombs or cannon fire on the water.
At 1200 B/6, HMS Unison was to the west-south-west of Marettimo in position 37°43'N, 11°29'E.
At 1205 B/6, a series of distant explosions was heard. (51)
7 May 1943 At 0450 B/7, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) dived and patrolled to the east from a point about 10 miles to the north of Raisi Point.

At 0820 B/7, smoke was sighted bearing 076°. Course was altered to close and investigate. The smoke proved to be coming from a hospital ship which eventually passed at a range of 3 miles to the north of HMS Unison. Enemy course was 290° at 12 knots. A dummy attack was made on this ship which could not be definitely identified but she looked like the Italian Principessa Giovanna (8389 GRT, built 1923).

Most likely this was indeed Principessa Giovanna which had arrived in Tunis at 2235 B/4 to embark wounded and sailed from there at 1245 B/5. At 1440 B/5, she was seriously damaged by American aircraft in position 37°15'N, 10°57'E, near the Island of Zembra. She arrived at Trapani at 1500 B/6 and sailed from there at 2115 B/6 and arrived in Naples at 00810 B/8.
At 1020 B/7, HMS Unison lost the hospital ship from sight on bearing 320°.
At 1200 B/7, HMS Unison was in position 38°22'N, 13°06'E.

At 1225 B/7, smoke was sighted on bearing 138°. This turned out to be another hospital ship, which passed to the south of HMS Unison on a course of 265° at 12 knots. This was a ship of about 3000 tons with a very pronounced clipper bow and one funnel. A dummy attack was also made on this vessel.

This was most likely the German hospital ship Konstanz (1459 GRT, built 1903) which had sailed from Messina at 2330 B/6 at 10 knots. On 8 May, it was decided that she would pick up wounded at Cape Farina (Tunisia). However, she arrived in Trapani only at 0700 B/9. It is possible that she went to Tunisia before.

Another hospital ship was in the area. The Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) had sailed from Naples at 2100 B/6 and arrived in Trapani at 1730 B/7. The time matches, but the description does not. (52)

8 May 19430043 B/8, in position 308° - Cape Gallo - 14.5 miles, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) heard HE (240 revs.) bearing 280°. HMS Unison was turned towards this bearing and a moment later a destroyer was seen coming towards.
At 0045 B/8, HMS Unison dived and shut off for depth charge attack. After diving the HE previously heard fafaded and the HE of a motor torpedo boat could be heard only.
By 0115 B/8, all HE had faded and no A/S impulses were heard.
At 0350 A/8, HMS Unison surfaced.
At 0440 A/8, HMS Unison dived for the day but nothing further was sighted all day. (3)

11 May 1943 At 0035 B/11, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted the lights of the Swedish relief ships Formosa (7033 GRT, built 1921) and Yarrawonga (4556 GRT, built 1941) bearing 340°. These vessels passed to the northward of their proper route on which HMS Unison had been informed.

At 0420 B/11, a motor torpedo boat was sighted about 2 miles to the northward proceeding on a course of 100° at slow speed. No HE was heard after diving and the vessel could also not be seen through the periscope. HMS Unison remained dived for the day.
At 1200 B/11, HMS Unison was to the north of Cape Calavà, in position 38°15'N, 14°54'E.

At 1727 B/11, smoke was sighted bearing 330°. Course was altered to 060° to close at full speed. A mast was seen of this vessel which was on an easterly course. This vessel, which was thought to be a destroyer or torpedo boat making about 25 knots, must have passed close to the south of Vulcano Island.
This was the Italian torpedo boat Clio which sailed from Trapani at 1115 B/11 and arrived in Messina at 2006 B/11. (3)

12 May 1943 At 2221 B/12, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1422 A/12, which contained the following orders; Leave patrol so as to pass position 'P' at 2359 A/14 thence to Malta as will be ordered by the Capt. S.10.

At 2340 B/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1917 B/12 was received which contained the following orders for P 43; Onward route from position 'P' through position 37°07'N, 13°15'E thence to make rendezvous with a minesweeper in position 36°08'N, 14° 22'E. (53)

13 May 1943 At 2211 B/13, north of Cape Gallo, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a ship bearing 190°. Course was altered to the same bearing and then to 120° and closed at full speed to attack. The enemy's course was shortly afterwards seen to be about 080° and at first it was thought to be a small merchant vessel. However after a few minutes when the range was about 5000 yards the vessel looked more like one of the enemy's 'Q' ships.

An AA gun pedestal could be seen amidships. Her appearance and the absence of any escort and the fact that her speed was judged to be a good 12 knots, now left Lt. Daniell no doubt that the enemy was indeed a 'Q' ship. The attack was abandoned.
[It is likely this was a vessel of the German 22nd UJ-Flotilla but unfortunately the KTB (logbook) for the period 1 to 15 May 1943 is not available.]
At 2349 B/13, an aircraft report was received of a medium sized vessel in position 38°24'N, 13°35'E at 2340 B/12 on course 090 at a speed of 15 knots.
At 2355 B/13, the lights of a hospital ship were sighted bearing 245°. It was later determined that this hospital ship was on a course of 090°.

At 0020 B/14, HMS Unison altered course to the southward to obtain a better view of this hospital ship which was subsequently seen to be a liner of about 7000 tons. Her lights were lost from sight at 0120 B/13.

[The German hospital ship Konstanz (1459 GRT, built 1903) was on passage from Trapani to Messina at the time of this sighting. She arrived at Messina around 2000 B/14 having sailed from Trapani at about 1730 B/13. (51)

14 May 1943 At 0807 B/14, near Cape San Vito, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a hospital ship bearing 250° proceeding on a course of 015°. This hospital ship passed at a range of about 5 miles and was identified as the Italian Sicilia (Italian, 9646 GRT, built 1924).
This was however the Italian hospital ship Toscana (9429 GRT, built 1923) which had sailed from Trapani at 0515 B/14 and reached Naples at 0655 B/15. (3)
17 May 1943 At 1110 B/17, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN), were swept in by a minesweeper [identity not known] which had joined around 0830 B/17. (3)

1 Jun 1943 Around 2055 B/1, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to conduct a special operation. Beach reconnaissance had to be carried out off Gela for which a Folbot with C.O.P.P personnel and one Chariot with Charioteers were embarked.
2 Jun 1943 HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) was off Gela, Sicily but the weather was not suitable for the beach reconnaissance to be carried out.
0420 B/2 - Dived and closed the beach for a periscope reconnaissance.
1445 B/2 - Sighted a schooner proceeding along the coast on a westerly course.

During the afternoon a north-westerly breeze sprang up so that by nightfall there was a sea and swell of 43. This was too much for launching the chariot and so the inshore reconnaissance had to be abandoned.
2132 B/2 - Surfaced and withdrew to seaward. (3)
3 Jun 1943 HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) was off Gela, Sicily but still the weather was not suitable for the beach reconnaissance to be carried out. It was decided to return to Malta.
0350 B/3 - Heard an aircraft going south.
0406 B/3 - Dived and closed the beach.
1200 B/3 - The wind had sea increased slightly during the day and the inshore reconnaissance by now had to be abandoned.
2143 B/3 - Withdrew from the coast and set course for Malta. The attempt had to be abandoned as the Chariot's battery could not be relied upon to last any longer. (3)
4 Jun 1943 Around 1350 B/4, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) returned to Malta.
She was escorted in by two minesweepers [unidentified] which had joined around 0815 B/4.
From 0958 B/4 to 1017 B/4, HMS Unison, while in company with the minesweeprs, dived due to an air raid warning. (3)
5 Jun 1943 Around 1655 B/5, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed Malta to resume her reconnaissance mission. (3)

6 Jun 1943 In the evening, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) launched a Chariot and a Folbot for beach reconnaissance of Gela, Sicily. However the Chariot sank immediately to the bottom due to a malfunction but the Folbot with C.O.P.P. personnel successfully carried out her mission.
0400 B/6 - Dived and closed the coast.
1330 B/6 - Sighted the wreck of a merchant vessel with two masts in position 37°01.7'N, 14°05.8'E.
1545 B/6 - Heard a distant explosion.
1800 B/6 - Sighted three fighters over Gela.
2130 B/6 - Surfaced. Weather conditions were ideal for launching the Chariot and Folbot. No swell and practically no sea.
2155 B/6 - Opened the fore hatch and the Folbot, C.O.P.P. party and the Charioteers came up.

2200 B/6 - Launched the Chariot and Folbot. The Chariot sank immediately when it was clear of the submarine and the Charioteers were unable to stay on it below 30 feet. It seems that the machine's trimming tanks had not been sufficiently pumped out before launching. All controls had been tested and found correct and these would not have functioned if the machine had been flooded. The Folbot was sent inshore with instructions not to approach within 200 yards of the beach.
0045 B/7 - The Folbot returned alongside and was re-embarked.
0056 B/7 - Set course to return to Malta. (3)
7 Jun 1943 Around 1645 B/7, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (3)

13 Jun 1943 Around 1800 B/13, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the south of the Straits of Messina.
14 Jun 1943 At 0430 B/14, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) entered zone 'T'.
Between 1248 B/14 and 1318 B/14, When near Cape Murro di Porco, 19 distant explosions were heard which sounded like bombs. (3)

15 Jun 1943 At 1430 B/15, when off Augusta, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted two laden motor schooners approaching Cape Campolato on a course of 155°. One was of about 100 tons, the other of about 70 tons. They kept very close inshore. They altered course to 175° and did not enter Augusta but disappeared in the direction of Panagia Bay.

At 2205 B/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1013 B/15 which contained the following orders for her; patrol in area T. south of 037° 20’ . Operate north of 37°25'N and west of 16°05'E after 0800 B/17. (54)
16 Jun 1943 HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Terni (former French Azrou, 2998 GRT, built 1931) off Catania, Sicily, Italy.

Terni, escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Ardimentoso and Orione (escort leader), had sailed from Naples at 2025 B/15-2100 B/15. Ardimentoso, equipped with sonar, scouted ahead until 0456 B/16 when she left the convoy and returned to Pozzuoli at 1106 B/16. At 2100 B/15-2115 B/15, the corvettes Driade and Persefone sailed from Messina and, at 0630 B/16, reinforced the escort in position 39°13'N, 14°43'30"E. At 1415 B/16, Orione received the order to the convoy to proceed to Syracuse. At 1425 B/16, Orione's No.1 boiler broke down due to a short circuit which caused a fire and the torpedo boat lagged behind but managed to rejoin the convoy at 1500 B/16. At 1904 B/16, Orione informed Persefone that her sonar had detected two suspicious echoes in position 37°19'30"N, 15°14'30"E and 37°20'N, 15°10'E. Two minutes later, Terni, stationed some 1500 metres astern of Orione, blew up. Orione asked Persefone if this was a mine or torpedo, but the reply was negative. At 1912 B/16, Driade signalled that the submarine was behind the steamer and on the east side. She had spotted the torpedoe's bubbles discharge and, at 1919 B/16, dropped three patterns of depth charges, followed by another twelve depth charges four minutes later. Large bubbles of air were observed, and the submarine was believed to be sunk, then joined the search for survivors. Orione had also attempted to pick up survivors but found none. She ordered the corvettes to hunt the submarine while she proceeded to Augusta. She arrived there at 2038 B/16. After the submarine attack, Persefone had dropped a few depth charges for intimidation purposes, but her sonar had broken down, so she looked for survivors, found ten of them (one died shortly after), and at 2040 B/16 left the scene for Augusta. She landed the survivors at 2120 B/16. At 2200 B/16, she received the order from Marina Messina to hunt the submarine and sailed again at 2300 B/16 and rejoined Driade at 0140 B/17. Driade had no more depth charges so she used her towing mine. After an unfruitful search, the two corvettes received an order from Marina Messina to return home, and they arrived in Messina at 1505 B/17-1512 B/17. Thirty-nine of the crew of the Terni perished as well as a number of her passengers.
1358 B/16 - Sighted a small steam gunboat slinking round the shores of Catania Bay towards Augusta. The vessel appeared to be towing a seaplane.
1822 B/16 - In position 37°26'N, 15°15'E sighted a funnel and smoke bearing 349°. Two ships could be seen but their course could not yet be made out.
1840 B/16 - Returned to periscope depth to have a second look. The enemy was seen to be a single merchant ship escorted by a destroyer ahead and two torpedo boats on either beam. Started attack.

1905 B/16 - In position 37°29'N, 15°13'E fired four torpedoes from 1000 yards. One torpedo hit was heard. This explosion was followed by a heavier one and then what was judged as another hit. At 1909 hours this was succeeded by a terrific concussion which severely shook the submarine (and her inmates) breaking about thirty lamps and bringing down from the pressure hull a rain of rust and cork-paint. This was the target blowing up.
1914 B/16 - A counter attack was started in which thirty depth charges were dropped but none were very close.

2012 B/16 - HE had faded and no more A/S transmissions were heard for the last 30 minutes. Returned to periscope depth. Sighted two torpedo boats about 4 miles astern heading to the east. Continued to withdraw to the north-east.
2328 B/16 - Surfaced to the north-eastward of the attack position. Nothing in sight. (3)

17 Jun 1943 At 0800 B/17, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) entered her new patrol area to the south of the Straits of Messina as had been ordered by the Capt. S.10 in his signal timed 1013 B/15.
Between 1111 B/17 and 1509 B/17, 15 distant explosions were heard. (3)
18 Jun 1943 At 0630 B/18, to the east of Cape dell'Armi, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a small schooner proceeding eastwards while hugging the coast.
At 0935 B/18, HMS Unison sighted two more small eastbound schooners hugging the coast. (3)

18 Jun 1943 In a signal timed 0830 B/18, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) and HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN); 1) United is to leave patrol to pass through position 37°23'N, 16°02'E at 2000 B/21 thence through positions 36°26'N, 15°43'E and 36° 26'N, 14°20'E to arrive at the Malta West Sanctuary rendezvous at 0830 B/23. 2) Unison is to leave patrol so as to follow the same route 24 hours later. (55)

19 Jun 1943 At 0520 B/19, to the east of Cape dell'Armi, in position 37°46.5'N, 15°49'E, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted the mast and upperworks of a destroyer bearing 170°. HMS Unison turned to the southwards to close and investigate. Very little more of this vessel could be seen but she looked similar to a Maestrale-class destroyer. This destroyer was steering an easterly course at slow speed and passed about 4 miles to the southward of the submarine. The destroyer disappeared from view at 0607 A/19.
This was most probably the Italian torpedo boat Calliope, which had sailed from Messina at 0312 B/19 and arrived in Taranto at 2055 B/19.

At 1201 B/19, while in position 37°55'N, 15°38'E, HMS Unison sighted two motor boat A/S vessels bearing 060°. They were proceeding along the coast in a north-westerly direction. These were fairly large boats with a big squat funnel, overhanging bow and with a sizeable gun forward. They looked like the 'pocket edition' of the single-funnelled Italian destroyers.
[We have been unable to identify these vessels.]
At 1320 B/19, several explosions were heard. Smoke and dust from the direction of Reggio di Calabria explained the cause of these, an Allied air bombardment,
At 2145 B/19, several distant explosions were heard. (3)

21 Jun 1943 At 0828 B/21, while patrolling to the east of Cape dell'Armi, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a small motor trawler going eastwards along the coast. This trawler remained stopped close inshore for about an hour and made off the the eastward later.

At 0840 B/21, a motor A/S vessel was sighted close inshore on a westerly course. This vessel was of the same type as those seen on the 19th. This vessel came towards but turned away and continued her course to the west when the range had come down to 3000 yards.
At 1202 B/21, when in position 37°52'N, 15°48'E an explosion was heard. (3)
22 Jun 1943 At 0353 B/22, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) dived for the day and commenced the return passage to Malta.

At 0524 B/22, in position 37°42'N, 15°52'E, a destroyer was sighted bearing 354°. The enemy was on an easterly course at slow speed. The destroyer was gradually turning towards HMS Unison though her courses were very erratic. The enemy never came closer than about 2 miles which was at 0545 B/22 when she suddenly altered course to 130° and increased speed considerably. At 0600 B/22 she was lost to sight on bearing 090°.

The Italian corvettes Persefone and Driade sailed from Messina for Syracuse at 0200 B/22 and it may have been one of them. They were to meet the Italian transport Alfieri (4573 GRT, built 1943), escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Ardito, which had sailed from Taranto at 0910 B/21 for Syracuse. The convoy had stopped in Crotone (1815 B/21-0443 B/22). At 0600 B/22, they were patrolling on the 38th parallel after being informed that the Ardito-convoy was two hours behind schedule (Ardito had a defective condenser). The convoy was finally sighted at 1100 B/22. They all proceeded to Syracuse. At 1655 B/22, Driade, which had trouble with her sonar, was detached to Taranto, arriving at 1350 B/23. At 1700 B/22, Alfieri was just about to enter Syracuse when an escorting aircraft fired bursts of machine-gun into the sea. Three minutes later, she was missed by four torpedoes fired from HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN), which exploded on the coast. The transport entered harbour while Ardito and Persefone carried out an A/S hunt. At 1845 B/22, The Italian transport Marconi (1188 GRT, built 1918) sailed from Syracuse for Messina. The two escort vessels were to escort her to Messina, but Persefone closed and ordered her to proceed alone by coastal route as they were busy hunting the submarine. The towing mine from the corvette exploded, but the sonar could not determine if it was a submarine. Nothing was detected until 2013 B/22, when Ardito obtained a good echo. Three minutes later, Persefone dropped three sets of depth charges, and the echo was no longer heard. Believing the submarine to have been disposed of, the two warships entered Augusta at 2215 B/22-2225 B/22.
Between 1430 B/22 and 2020 B/22, about 45 distant explosions were heard.
This may have had something to do with the hunt and depth charging of HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) near Cape Murro di Porco. (3)
24 Jun 1943 Around 1140 B/24, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Beryl (T/Lt. H. Olsberg, RNR) which had joined around 0745 B/24. (3)

6 Jul 1943 At 2100 B/6, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed from Malta to act as beacon for operation Husky ('Bark South' Assault area), the invasion of Sicily. This is her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean).
[As no log is available no map can be displayed.] (56)
9 Jul 1943 At 2300 B/9, near Cape Passero, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) began transmitting for the invasion fleet in her sector.

10 Jul 1943 At 0011 B/10, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN), sighted the first convoy and, her duty done, returned to Malta escorted by the armed trawler HMS Gavotte (T/Lt. D. Bates, RNR). Gavotte had joined shortly after 0600 B/10 and parted company around 1125 B/10.
HMS Unison arrived at Malta around 1405 B/10. (56)

17 Jul 1943 Around 1200 B/17, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of the Straits of Messina.
She departed Malta together with HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN). The submarines were escorted until around 0600 B/18 to the west of Pantelleria by HMS Swona (T/Lt. E.C. Davids, RNR).
HMS Unison was to proceed through positions 37°03'N, 11°51'E, 37°32'N, 11°50'E, 38°00'N, 11°51'E, 38°19'N, 12°20'E to position 38°22'N, 12°57'E at 2000 B/19.
Further instructions would be signalled while on passage to the patrol area. (57)
17 Jul 1943 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unison during her 18th war patrol see the map below.

19 Jul 1943 In a signal timed 1210 B/19 the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR); Patrol areas north of Messina allocated as follows; Unsparing north of 38°33'N and west of 15°30'E. Unrivalled north of 38°33'N, and east of 15°30'E. Unison south of 38°33'N. Areas are bound by a circle with a radius of 17 miles from position 38°33'N, 15°30'E. (58)
19 Jul 1943 At 0533 B/19, north-west of Trapani, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted two motor torpedo boats on a westerly course.
At 0735 B/19, two distant explosions were heard. (3)
20 Jul 1943 At 0123 B/20, north-east of Palermo Bay, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) the wake of a motor torpedo boat was sighted. HMS Unison dived to avoid, surfacing again at 0222 B/20.
At 0207 B/20, a distant explosion had bean heard. (3)

21 Jul 1943 At 0050 B/21, in position 38°24'N, 15°10'E, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) a white light was sighted bearing 080°. This was soon seen to be the mast-head light of a hospital ship on course 260°.
At 0140 B/21, the hospital ship was still about 5 miles away when it altered course to 180° to port and disappeared in the direction from where it had come.
At 0800 B/21, between Cape Milazzo and the northern entrance to the Strait of Messina, three motor torpedo boats were sighted proceeding westwards close inshore.

At 0938 B/21, two motor torpedo boats were sighted bearing 209°. These were proceeding eastwards. They looked exactly like U-boats on sighting and a serious attack was in progress until were identified as motor torpedo boats.
At 1200 B/21, HMS Unison was in position 38°22'N, 15°34'E.

At 1453 B/21, a hospital ship was sighted coming out of the Straits of Messina. A dummy attack was carried out on this northbound hospital ship. It was the Italian Toscana (9429 GRT, built 1923). The name could be read when she passed. She made off on a course of 280°.
Toscana had sailed from Ganzirri (near Messina) at 1400 B/21 and arrived in Gaeta at 1750 B/22.
At 1800 B/21, a number of distant explosions were heard. (3)

21 Jul 1943 In a signal timed 1205 B/21, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) and HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR); Take up following positions on the 22nd from the centre of the circle referred to in my 1210 B/19 at times indicated; Unrivalled 333° - 17 miles at 0200 B/22. Unsparing 270° - 9 miles at 0300 B/22. Unison 219° - 17 miles at 0400 B/22. [Corrupt] in a southerly direction 26 miles. To normal patrol after 1200 B/22, Unison keep to southward of her area. (59)

22 Jul 1943 HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN), off Cape Milazzo, in position, 38°20'N, 15°16'E, fired four torpedoes at a 3000 ton merchant vessel escorted by two torpedo boats. They all missed. This must have been the Italian transport Alfieri (4573 GRT, built 1943) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Partenope and Ardimentoso, which were on passage from Naples to Milazzo.
The convoy had sailed from Naples/Pozzuoli at 1929 B-1950 B/21. Ardimentoso was the convoy leader.
At 0100 B/2, the Metox of Ardimentoso detected an aircraft and five minutes later, Alfieri was attacked by a bomber which dropped four bombs and missed her.

At 0730 B/22, three torpedoes missed Partenope ahead, followed at 0738.5B/22 by a fourth. Ardimentoso’s sonar had not detected anything as it was defective. Ardimentoso ordered Partenope to hunt the submarine while she escorted Alfieri to Milazzo where they arrived at 0810 B/22. Ardimentoso had ordered the corvette Camoscio to join Partenope in the chase, and at 0800 B/22, she sailed from Milazzo.

At 0835 B/22, the Italian transport Tivoli (former French La Marsa, 1495 GRT, built 1914) sailed from Milazzo for Messina, escorted by Ardimentoso and the corvette Cicogna. At 1225 B/22, they arrived in Messina. Cicogna was ordered to join Partenope and Camoscio in the submarine hunt.

In the meantime, at 0750 B/22, Partenope’s sonar detected an echo at a range of 500 metres. At 0811 B/22, she dropped nine depth charges, the first two set at a depth of 50 metres and the remainder at 90 metres. No results were observed.

At 0825 B/22, an echo was detected at 300 metres and three depth charges, set at 90 metres, were delivered. After the first, boiling water was observed, and a strong smell of oil but no debris and it was believed the submarine was damaged.
At 0845 B/22, the corvette Camoscio joined.
At 0909 B/22, a new movement was detected by the sonar at 300 metres, and four depth charges were dropped at 90 metres. Again, boiling water was observed.
At 0944 B/22, her sonar had broken down, Partenope left the area for Messina, leaving the hunt to be carried out by Camoscio.

At 1240 B/22, she sighted the corvette Cicogna, which was on her way to join Camoscio. [No report was located of Camoscio and Cicogna for their A/S search, but Supermarina makes no mention so we can assume that it was negative.]
At 2022 B/22, Partenope arrived in Messina. She was followed by Camoscio at 2050 B/22 and Cicogna at ???? [document missing].
0158 B/22 - Set course for position 38°20'N, 15°16'E as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1205 B/21.
0230 B/22 - Dived to lessen the chance of detection while approaching the coast.
0341 B/22 - Surfaced.
0400 B/22 - Arrived in the ordered position. Dived shortly afterwards.
0705 B/22 - Sighted one merchant ship bearing 005° at a range of about 6.5 miles. This ship was escorted by two torpedo boats. Commenced attack. Enemy course was 180°.

0713 B/22 - The target had now zigged to 200°. Unison was running out to the eastward to gain distance off track. Ten minutes later the target was back on course 180°. Enemy speed was thought to be 15 knots. It looked as though Unison might not get clear to the eastward of the enemy's zig to port, which took place at 0732 B/22. However when the enemy transport did the zig, Unison was still 6° on the enemy's port bow and a retiring turn was made in the hope of firing on a broad track.
0736 B/22 - A quick look through the periscope showed that the target had now altered 40° to starboard to a course of 200°. A quick turn had to be made in order to steady HMS Unison on a 135° track.

0738 B/22 - Fired four torpedoes from 2000 yards. The two escorts, one on either bow of the target had not zigged during the attack. No torpedoes hit the target and it was realised that the depth set to them (16 feet) had been too much. The target was thought to be about 3000 tons and was not laden. Perhaps just a little more than in ballast.
During the A/S hunt that followed HMS Unison went to 120 feet and retired to the northward, leaving the torpedo boats astern. 28 Depth charges were counted but none fell close.
1055 B/22 - Returned to periscope depth as no depth charges had been dropped for about an hour. The two torpedo boats were about 4 miles away bearing 170°.
1102 B/22 - Went to 60 feet and proceeded to the eastward.
1200 B/22 - HMS Unison was in position 37°27'N, 15°23'E.
1648 B/22 - The HE of the torpedo boats had been lost and on coming to periscope depth all was clear.
1900 B/22 - Commenced reloading the torpedo tubes.
2149 B/22 - Surfaced. (3)
23 Jul 1943 At 0030 B/23, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) heard faint HE bearing 295°. Nothing could be seen on this bearing though. The HE faded a few minutes later.
At 0208 B/23, HMS Unison dived for the day.

At 0330 B/23, HE was heard on bearing 130° and 17 minutes later a torpedo boat was sighted bearing 140°. This vessel remained in the vicinity until daylight but must have been unaware of the submarines presence because no depth charges were dropped.
This was probably the corvette Gabbiano, which sailed from Messina at 1845 B/22 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1000 B/23.
At 0532 B/23, a number of fairly distant explosions were heard.

At 0611 B/23, a torpedo boat was sighted bearing 073°. This vessel approached HMS Unison but took no offensive action and was lost to sight at 0750 B/23. It was decided to close Milazzo where it was thought yesterday's merchant vessel had gone to. It was hoped it would be possible to attack it through the harbour entrance.
Probably this was Gabbiano once more.
At 1200 B/23, HMS Unison was in position 38°19'N, 15°21'E.

At 1500 B/23, a periscope observation of the harbour was completed. The merchant vessel was not seen along the east end of the north mole so an attack through the harbour entrance was out of the question.
At 1508 B/23, a small tug was seen to round Cape Milazzo from the westward. It entered the harbour at 1520 B/23. (3)

23 Jul 1943 In a signal timed 1529 B/23, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) and HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR); 1) Unsparing is to leave leave patrol routed through positions 38°45'N, 15°12'E at 0200 B/26, 38°17'N, 10°49'E at 1200 B/28, 37°42'N, 09°33'E at 0600 B/29 to make rendezvous with an escort vessel flying a black flag in position 37°26'N, 09°33'E at 1200 B/29. 2) Unison is to move to Unsparing's area at 0300 B/26. 3) Unrivalled and Unison are to leave patrol by the same route as above 24 hours later. Unrivalled is to keep 2 miles north and Unison 2 miles south of the route. An escort will be proviced at 1200 B/30. (58)
24 Jul 1943 At 0344 B/24, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) sighted a ship bearing 170°. This was subsequently seen to be a motor torpedo boat laying stopped.
At 0348 B/24, HMS Unison dived and patrolled from Cape Rasocolmo to the north of the Straits of Messina.
At 0500 B/24, the lights of a hospital ship were sighted bearing 310°. This was seen to be the Toscana (9429 GRT, built 1923) which entered the Straits of Messina.
This was actually Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928), which sailed from Gaeta at 1425 B/23 and arrived in Messina at 0810 B/24.
At 0823 B/24, seven distant explosions were heard.
At 1200 B/24, HMS Unison was in position 38°21'N, 15°30'E.
At 1242 B/24, a series of distant explosions was heard.
Around 1425 B/24, the Toscana came out from the Straits of Messina. She returned into the Straits around 1930 B/24.
At 1810 B/24, a motor torpedo boat passed to the eastward on a course of 020°.
At 2147 B/24, HMS Unison surfaced.
Around 2153 B/24, the Toscana came out once more of the Straits and proceeded to the westward. (3)
25 Jul 1943 At 1200 B/25, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) was in position 38°20'N, 15°38'E.

At 1239 B/25, the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) was seen to leave the Straits of Messina on a northerly course. She cruised around at slow speed and eventually made off on a course of 260°. She was lost to sight at 1615 B/25.

This was indeed the Virgilio which according to Supermarina Diaries sailed from Messina at 1400 B/24 (she may have left the area at 1400 B/24, which could explain Unison sighting her before). She returned to Messina at 0630 B/25. She may have stayed out of the harbour due to the Allied bombing.
At 1432 B/25, a motor torpedo boat was seen close inshore proceeding on a course of 210° making for the Strait of Messina. This motor torpedo boat was lost to sight at 1458 B/25.
At 1511 B/25, four motor torpedo boats were seen proceeding on the same course as the motor torpedo boat seen at 1432 B/25.
At 1900 B/25, a motor A/S boat was seen proceeding northwards.
At 1941 B/25, then landing ships, thought to be Siebel ferries, were seen proceeding northwards keeping close inshore. (3)
26 Jul 1943 At 0300 B/26, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) entered the north-west patrol area as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1520 B/23. (3)
27 Jul 1943 At 0145 B/27, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) left patrol and set course 265° at 10 knots in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1529 B/23. (3)

27 Jul 1943 In a signal timed 2202 B/27, the Commander-in-Chief, Mediterranean, sent the following orders to HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) and HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN); Amend route as given in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1529 B/23 to pass 5 miles to the north of Ustica before proceeding to position 38°17'N, 10°49'E. (60)

30 Jul 1943 Around 1415 B/30, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) arrived at Bizerta together with HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN). They had to wait there for onward passage to Malta in the next convoy.
They were escorted in from shortly before 1200 B/30, by HMS Foula (T/Lt.Cdr. E.R.O.C. Greenstreet, RNVR). (3)

2 Aug 1943 Around 1500 B/2, HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) departed Bizerta together with HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) to join convoy KMS 21 for onward passage to Malta. They were escorted by HM HDML 1251 (T/Lt. T.K. Yates, RNVR) to the rendezvous. Also one merchant vessel that was to join the convoy was in company.

Late in the evening, shortly after joining the convoy, both submarines were taken under fire by one of ships of the convoy, the American tanker Yankee Arrow (8046 GRT, built 1921) which had apparently not been informed about their presence.

Unison was hit on the conning tower and the pressure hull. The Officer of the Watch, T/Lt. J.P. King, RNR was killed and the Commanding Officer, Lt. Daniell, DSC, RN and the two lookouts, petty officer Day and leading seaman Halliday were wounded.
Both submarines then returned to Bizerta escorted by ORP Slazak (Kmdr.ppor. (Cdr.) R. Nalecz-Tyminski). (3)

3 Aug 1943 After having returned to Bizerta, HMS Unison, now commanded by her 1st Lieutenant Lt. J.E.E.D. Haward, RN, underwent repairs while laying alongside HMS Abercrombie (A/Capt. G.V.B. Faulkner, RN). When this ship had to proceed to sea she shifted to HMS Vindictive (Rear Admiral (retd.) G.R.S. Watkins, RN) to continue emergency repairs alongside this ship.

During the morning Lt. Haward visited the American hospital where the casualties were still being operated on or were still under the effects of the anaesthetics. Also T/Lt. King was buried at sea with Naval Honours from HM HDML 1241 (T/Lt. A.D. Kingsford, RNVR)
In the evening the hospital was again visited. Lt. Daniell and petty officer Day were in good spirits but leading seaman Halliday was still asleep. (3)

4 Aug 1943 After emergency repairs, HMS Unison (Lt. J.E.E.D. Haward, RN) departed Bizerta for diving trials. After these had been completed she and HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) departed Bizerta around 1100 B/4, for passage to Malta escorted by HMS Clacton (A/Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) L.S. Shaw, RNR).
Around 0730 B/5, the armed yacht HMS Charles McIver (Lt. R.T. Patterson, RANVR) took over the escort duties. (61)
5 Aug 1943 Around 1500 B/5, HMS Unison (Lt. J.E.E.D. Haward, RN) arrived at Malta from Bizerta.

Full repairs were made there and these were completed by 18 August 1943. It had been intended to sent Unison on patrol in the Adriatic for a last patrol in the Mediterranean before she was due to return to the U.K. to refit in early September.

There was now not enough time left to do a full patrol and it was decided that Unison would run A/S exercises for destroyers based on Malta. She did so for three days while awaiting her passage to the U.K. This included an exercise with HMS Wheatland (Lt.Cdr. R. de L. Brooke, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN) using the Pillenwerfer seized on the Italian submarine Bronzo. (62)
8 Sep 1943 Around 1030 B/8, HMS Unison (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) departed Malta for passage to the U.K.

She was in company with HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) which was also to return to the U.K. The submarines were escorted to the rendezvous with convoy MKS 24 by HMS Bergamot (Lt. R.T. Horan, RNR).
Around 1530 B/8, off Malta, the submarines and corvette joined convoy MKS 24.
[The convoy report of this convoy does not seem to have survived. So far we have been unable to find it in the archives. Further research is required.] (3)
8 Sep 1943 For the daily positions of HMS Unison during the passage from Malta to Gibraltar see the map below.
10 Sep 1943 At 0900 B/10, HMS Unison (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) sighted the Italian Battlefleet on her way to surrender at Malta. (3)
13 Sep 1943 Around 2000 B/13, HMS Unison (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Malta. (3)
16 Sep 1943 Around 1045 B/16, HMS Unison (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) departed Gibraltar for Plymouth. She was deemed ready for patrol only in an emergency.

On leaving Gibraltar, HMS Unison was routed as follows; Position 'B', 35°49'N, 06°06'W at 1520 B/16. Position 'V', 35°53'N, 06°15'W at 1630 B/16. Position 'T', 36°37'N, 07°24'W at 0201 B/17. Position 'Q', 36°51'N, 09°13'W at 0030 B/18. Position 'M', 38°40'N, 10°28'W at 0230 B/19 and Position 'S', 43°01'N, 10°28'W at 1400 B/21. Further onward routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.).
At 1845 B/16, orders were received (Flag Officer in Command Gibraltar's signal timed 1401 B/16) to patrol between 2130 B/16 and 0130 B/17, 30 miles to the south-west of Cadiz.
The passage to the U.K. now thus became her 19th war patrol.
In a signal timed 1539 B/16, the sailing orders were amended and HMS Unison was now to pass through position 'T' at 0700 B/17, 'Q' at 0500 B/18, 'M' at 0710 B/19 and 'S' at 0001 B/22.

16 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1809 A/16, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Unison (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) and HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN); 1) From position 'S' (43°01'N, 10°28'W), HMS Unison proceed dived by day via route 'Rose' to make rendezvous with the escort in position 'YY', 180° - Lizard - 10 miles at 0900 A/27 thence to Plymouth. 2) HMS United is to proceed from position 'O' (43°01'N, 10°01'W), dived by day via route 'Gold' to point 'Q' thence direct to carry out an anti-Uboat patrol in zone 'J 7'. Onward route will be signalled later. 3) Bombing restrictions will follow. (64)

17 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1708 B/17, the Flag Officer in Command Gibraltar informed HMS Unison (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) and HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) of the following; Enemy submarine sighted by aircraft (a Hudson of 233 Squadron based in Gibraltar) in position 37°43'N, 09°33'W at 1344 B/17 on the surface and observed to dive. Probably southbound.
[We can not place a German U-boat in this (approximate) position at this time.] (64)

21 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 2105 A/21, HMS Unison (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) sent the following signal to the Flag Officer Submarines and the Flag Officer in Command Gibraltar; Passing position 'S' (43°01'N, 10°28'W) at 1200 A/22 owing to bad weather. E.T.A. in position 'YY' (180° - Lizard - 10 miles) 24 hours late. (64)

25 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1758 A/25, the Flag Officer Submarines sent the following orders to HMS Unison (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN); 1) Adjust speed to make rendezvous with Chasseur 14 in position 'YY' (180° - Lizard - 10 miles) at 1330 A/28. My 1809 A/16 and your 2105 A/21 refer. 2) Proceed on the surface at your discretion north of lat. 49°30'N. (65)

26 Sep 1943 At 0435 A/26, in position 47°00'N, 06°42'W, sighted the lights of three vessels ahead. The were later identified as two masted fishing vessels. Although they were west of the prescribed fishing limints and therefore liable to be sunk they looked innocent and were not molested with. They were not torpedo targets and the weather was not very suitable for gun action. HMS Unison passed one mile to the westward of them. (3)
28 Sep 1943 Around 2210 A/28, HMS Unison (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) arrived at Plymouth.
She had arrived at the rendezvous position ('YY', 180° - Lizard - 10 miles) with her escort at 1300 A/28 but the escort was not there.
At 1420 A/28, the escort, HMS Northcotes (T/Lt. A.G. Hallows, RNVR), arrived and they proceeded in company to Plymouth. (3)
20 Oct 1943 HMS Unison (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) was taken in hand for refit at the Devonport Dockyard.

[As no logs are available for the period from her arrival at Plymouth until she was taken in hand for refit we do not have info on her deployment while at Plymouth. Possibly she participated in exercises.] (66)
3 Mar 1944 HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) is wiped (degaussed) at Plymouth. (67)
4 Mar 1944 HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) conducted D/G trials at Plymouth. (67)
5 Mar 1944 HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) conducted gunnery trials off Plymouth. (67)

6 Mar 1944 Having completed her refit at the Devonport Dockyard, HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR), conducted exercises off Plymouth. During these exercises she was escorted by HMS Ambrose Pare (Skr. J.W. Morris, RNR). (67)

8 Mar 1944 Around 0830 A/8, HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) and HMS United (Lt. N.R. Wood, RN) departed Plymouth for Rothesay. They were escorted HMS Herschell (T/Lt. J.E. Freestone, RNR) and HMS Daffodil (Cdr.(Retd.) S.F. Russell, RN). (67)
10 Mar 1944 Around 1130 A/10, HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) and HMS United (Lt. N.R. Wood, RN) arrived at Rothesay from Plymouth. (68)
17 Mar 1944 HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) conducted night gunnery exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Blade (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR) as escort. (67)

19 Mar 1944 Around 1500 A/19, HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) departed Rothesay for Blyth. She made the passage north through the Minches together with HMS Ursula (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN). They were escorted by HMS Pennywort (T/Lt. A.W. James, RNR). (67)
21 Mar 1944 At 0930 A/21, HMS Lord Ashfield (T/Lt. J.B. Morpeth, RNR), took over the escort of HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) for onward passage to Blyth. (67)
22 Mar 1944 Around 1245 A/22, HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) arrived at Blyth from Rothesay. She was now assigned to training duties. (67)
25 Mar 1944 HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) conducted exercises off Blyth. (67)

[No log of May 1944 is available for HMS Unison so some details for this month may be missing.] (69)
5 May 1944 HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) departed Blyth for Rosyth. (66)

30 May 1944 HMS Unison (T/Lt. P.C.S. Pritchard, RNR) was decommissioned on this date and loaned to the USSR. (70)
